text: In shift-reduce parsing workflows, two distinct parser states admit exactly one valid executable action: first, when the parser stack holds fewer than two elements, and second, when the input token queue is empty. Under both of these conditions, the probability assigned to the only permitted action is set to 1, which introduces an undesirable bias in our model toward sequences that contain a large number of non-optional processing stages. To address this problem, we employ an adjusted formulation for calculating sequence probabilities during beam search inference. Our modified beam search procedure retains the standard mechanism of preserving the top-k highest-scoring partial sequences across decoding steps, but computes the overall sequence score as the average of the predicted probabilities of selected actions exclusively for decision steps where more than one candidate action was available.
source_text: When performing the shift-reduce parsing, two kinds of states have only one action to choose: (1) less than two elements in the stack, and (2) no element in the queue. Under the above two conditions, the probability of the selected action will be 1, making our model to be overly biased on those sequences having many non-optional stages. For this reason, we apply an alternative way to compute the sequence probability during beam search. Our modified beam search is still fulfilled by maintaining the top k sequences, but the score of a sequence is calculated by the average probabilities of the selected actions that have more than one choice.

text: For experiments conducted on the Multi30K dataset, we report results using the METEOR metric (Banerjee & Lavie, 2005), consistent with evaluation protocols established in prior related work. For experiments run on the Conceptual Captions dataset, we observe that standard automated image captioning evaluation metrics—including BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002), ROUGE (Lin, 2004), CIDEr (Vedantam et al., 2015), and SPICE (Anderson et al., 2016)—fail to reliably measure captioning accuracy for non-English target languages. Nevertheless, we report CIDEr scores for baseline comparability, and contrast these automated outputs with results from structured human evaluation. The custom human evaluation framework deployed for this analysis is described in the subsequent section.
source_text: In the experiments done using the Multi30K dataset, we are reporting results using the ME-TEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005) metric, in line with previous work. For the experiments performed using the Conceptual Captions dataset, we have found that automated evaluation metrics for im-age captioning such as BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002) , ROUGE (Lin, 2004) , CIDEr (Vedantam et al., 2015) , and SPICE (Anderson et al., 2016) cannot accurately measure captioning accuracy for non-English languages. However, we are reporting CIDEr numbers as a point of comparison, and contrast these numbers with human evaluation results. We describe the human evaluation framework we use next.

text: For the DiCoh model, the hidden state dimension of the long short-term memory (LSTM) components is set to 128 for the utterance module and 256 for the dialogue module. Model parameters are optimized using the Adam optimizer, with all default Adam hyperparameters retained except for the initial learning rate, which is set to 0.0005. A dropout layer configured with p = 0.1 is applied to all utterance vectors. The model is trained for 20 epochs on the DailyDialog corpus and 10 epochs on the SwitchBoard corpus, with performance evaluated on the validation set at the end of each epoch. The model checkpoint that achieves the highest validation set performance is selected for final evaluation on the held-out test set. Parameters γ₁ and γ₂ (see Equation 11) are initialized to 2.0 and updated dynamically throughout training. To ensure fair comparison across experiments, all comparative models are trained and evaluated on identical training, validation, and test splits.
source_text: In DiCoh, the size of the hidden states in LSTMs of the utterance module is 128 and of the dialogue module is 256. The parameters of this model are optimized using the Adam optimizer where its parameters have default values except the learning rate which is initiated with 0.0005. A dropout layer with p = 0.1 is applied to the utterance vectors. We train the model for 20 epochs on DailyDialog and 10 epochs on SwitchBoard and evaluate it at the end of each epoch on the validation set. The best performing model on the validation set is used for the final evaluation on the test set. Parameters γ 1 and γ 2 (see Equation 11) are initiated with 2.0 and are updated during training. To have fair comparisons, we train and evaluate all compared models on identical training, validation, and test sets.

text: Discourse relations characterize the logical connections holding between two contiguous textual spans, which may take the form of individual clauses, standalone sentences, or larger multi-sentence segments. Standard formalizations of discourse relation instances frame each relation as structured around a connective that takes two ordered arguments, conventionally labelled Arg1 and Arg2 respectively. A particularly persistent open challenge in the field of discourse analysis is implicit discourse relation recognition, the task of identifying relations when no explicit connective is present (Pitler et al., 2009), which requires inferring the underlying logical link exclusively from the surrounding contextual information. Progress on this task delivers concrete benefits to a broad suite of downstream natural language processing (NLP) applications, including machine translation (Meyer and Popescu-Belis, 2012) and text summarization (Gerani et al., 2014).
source_text: Discourse relation describes how two adjacent text units (e.g., clauses, sentences, and larger sentence groups) are connected logically to one another. A discourse relation instance is usually defined as a connective taking two arguments (as Arg1 and Arg2, respectively). Implicit discourse relation recognition without explicit connectives (Pitler et al., 2009) is still a challenging problem of discourse analysis, which needs to infer the discourse relation from a specific context. It is beneficial to many downstream natural language processing (NLP) applications, such as machine translation (Meyer and Popescu-Belis, 2012 ) and text summarization (Gerani et al., 2014) .

text:
1. Test suite construction
Non-technical users can leverage SyntaxGym’s browser-native interface to design and submit their custom psycholinguistic test suites (Figure 3). In parallel, the platform supports upload of pre-built test suites in JSON file format, a functionality intended for advanced users who prefer to generate test suites through automated workflows.
2. Language model submission
Users wishing to evaluate their own custom language models first produce a public Docker image that conforms to the API defined in the SyntaxGym standard (Section 3.1.1). Following submission of these language models via the SyntaxGym site, the platform’s backend automatically validates that the models meet the required API specifications. Validated models are added to the public SyntaxGym model collection, and will be run against all existing and future test suites available in the repository.
source_text: Create test suites Non-technical users can use SyntaxGym's browser-based interface to design and submit their own psycholinguistic test suites (Figure 3) . Separately, the site supports uploading pre-made test suites as a JSON-formatted file. This functionality may be useful for advanced users who prefer to automatically generate test suites. 2 Submit language models Users interested in evaluating their own language models first create a public Docker image conforming to the API specified by the SyntaxGym standard (Section 3.1.1). After users submit these language models on the SyntaxGym site, the models are automatically validated for conformity to the API by the SyntaxGym backend. Valid models are added to the SyntaxGym collection, and will be evaluated on all past and future available test suites in the Gym.

text: The En-Zh alignment matrix exhibits greater divergence overall, with only 65% of its alignment edges linking tokens that share the same part of speech (POS). A full 11% of English nouns are translated as Chinese verbs, while the inverse mapping of verbs to nouns is observed across all three corpora, though to a far lesser degree. Most of these cross-POS cases involve labels for actions and agents, including examples like borrowing and ruler. English negative particles are split across Chinese adverbs, verbs, and auxiliaries in translation; adjectives are very frequently mapped to Chinese nouns that serve as components of compound terms, as seen in the example of social media rendered as shèjiāo méitǐ, with the literal gloss “social-interaction media”. Spatial adpositions, the only category of adpositions classified as content words for this analysis, are predominantly mapped to Chinese adverbs.
source_text: The En-Zh matrix presents more divergences with only 65% of the alignment edges connecting tokens with the same POS. 11% of nouns were translated as verbs (the reverse mapping is found, albeit to a lesser extent, in all three corpora). Most of such cases involve names of actions and agents (borrowing, ruler, etc.). En negative particles are split between Zh adverbs, verbs, and auxiliaries; adjectives are quite often mapped to nouns, which form parts of compounds (e.g., social media → shèjiāo méitǐ, lit. 'social-interaction media'). Adpositions involving spatial relations (the only type of adpositions we consider as content words) are predominantly mapped to adverbs.

text: While replicating all of our experiments and computational cost estimations using state-of-the-art fine-tuning models including BERT, XLNet, RoBERTa, and ALBERT would carry considerable methodological value, these transformer-based architectures incur prohibitively high training overheads—as explicitly documented by the original BERT authors in the official BERT GitHub repository (reference 21)—and exceed the capacity of our current computational infrastructure, placing such extensions beyond the scope of the present work. Even so, ELMo contextualized word embeddings remain a highly useful modeling framework that delivers an exceptionally favorable performance-to-training-cost tradeoff; notably, ELMo sets new state-of-the-art scores on parsing and part-of-speech (POS) tagging tasks across the five languages selected for our analysis, outperforming even the multilingual mBERT model on these benchmarks.
source_text: Even though it would have been interesting to replicate all our experiments and computational cost estimations with state-of-the-art fine-tuning models such as BERT, XLNet, RoBERTa or AL-BERT, we recall that these transformer-based architectures are extremely costly to train, as noted by the BERT authors on the official BERT GitHub repository, 21 and are currently beyond the scope of our computational infrastructure. However we believe that ELMo contextualized word embeddings remain a useful model that still provide an extremely good trade-off between performance to training cost, even setting new state-of-the-art scores in parsing and POS tagging for our five chosen languages, performing even better than the multilingual mBERT model.

text: This work introduces a novel dialogue coherence model with utterance encoder layers that are jointly shared with a dialogue act prediction model. In contrast to prior methodologies that deploy these two models sequentially in a pipeline architecture, we employ them within a multi-task learning framework in which dialogue act prediction functions as the auxiliary task. The proposed coherence method outperforms competing baseline approaches at distinguishing intact dialogues from their various generated perturbations on the DailyDialog corpus, and delivers largely comparable performance to these baselines on the SwitchBoard corpus. The proposed architecture exhibits two core benefits: (a) it leverages the dialogue act prediction task during training to derive semantically informative utterance vectors, and (b) it reduces the requirement for gold-standard dialogue act labels during evaluation. Taken together, these properties render our model well-suited for benchmarking distinct dialogue agents along the dimensions of conversational coherence and naturalness. For future work, we intend to conduct an in-depth investigation of the effects of our perturbation strategies on the coherence of the evaluated dialogues. We will additionally examine the degree to which dialogue rankings generated by our model correlate with corresponding human-annotated rankings.
source_text: We propose a novel dialogue coherence model whose utterance encoder layers are shared with a dialogue act prediction model. Unlike previous approaches that utilize these two models in a pipeline, we use them in a multi-task learning scenario where dialogue act prediction is an auxiliary task. Our coherence method outperforms its counterparts for discriminating dialogues from their various perturbations on DailyDialog, and (mostly) performs on par with them on SwitchBoard. Our model (a) benefits from dialogue act prediction task during training to obtain informative utterance vectors, and (b) alleviates the need for gold dialogue act labels during evaluations. These properties holistically make our model suitable for comparing different dialogue agents in terms of coherence and naturalness. For future work, we would like to deeply study the impacts of our perturbations on the coherence of the examined dialogues. We will also investigate to what extent the rankings of dialogues obtained by our model correlate with human-provided rankings.

text: Consequence-based analysis will be deployed on naturalistic rather than template-generated data, integrated into high-level tasks such as question answering to address the unnaturalness limitation and empirically validate a model’s real-world utility. The risk of spurious side-channel information leakage is well-documented for this class of high-level tasks (e.g., Poliak et al., 2018; Geva et al., 2019), and a broad range of challenge datasets have been developed to target and reduce this bias across varied task contexts (Levesque et al., 2011; Chao et al., 2017; Dua et al., 2019; Lin et al., 2019). Combining these resources with a curated corpus of linguistically challenging sentence stimuli (e.g., Marvin & Linzen, 2018; Warstadt et al., 2019) to construct a syntax-centric question answering challenge benchmark would yield novel empirical insights into which model families successfully capture the functional advantages of authentic hierarchical syntactic representation.
source_text: Consequence-based analysis would be implemented over naturalistic data rather than templates by embedding it in higher level tasks like question answering to mitigate the unnaturalness problem and demonstrate a model's practical utility. The possibility of side-channel information is already known in relation to these higher-level tasks (e.g., Poliak et al., 2018; Geva et al., 2019) , and various challenge data sets have been constructed to mitigate it in different ways (Levesque et al., 2011; Chao et al., 2017; Dua et al., 2019; Lin et al., 2019; . Uniting these with a collection of hard sentence types (e.g., Marvin and Linzen, 2018; Warstadt et al., 2019) in something like a syntax-focused QA challenge set would provide new insights into which families of models capture the practical benefits of true hierarchical syntactic representation.

text: While a subset of targeted syntactic evaluations, including the example outlined above, rely on straightforward comparisons of word conditional probabilities within their preceding context, other such evaluation paradigms are substantially more complex. This can be illustrated via a paradigm designed to assess model performance on “garden-path” sentence processing (Futrell et al., 2019). For instance, the sentence *The child kicked in the chaos found her way back home* elicits measurable processing difficulty in human readers when they encounter the word found. This effect arises because, immediately preceding the disambiguating word found, the part-of-speech ambiguous verb kicked is preferentially parsed as the main verb of the clause, when in actuality it functions as a passive participle in a reduced relative clause modifying the head noun child. This garden-path disambiguation cost is reduced when kicked is replaced with forgotten, a verb with no part-of-speech ambiguity (condition B below; Trueswell et al., 1994), or when an unreduced relative clause structure is used instead (condition C below; Ferreira and Clifton, 1986). In probabilistic language models, these garden-path disambiguation effects are reliably captured by word-level negative log probabilities, formally termed SURPRISAL (Hale, 2001): \(S(w|C) = -\log_2 p(w|C)\), a metric that is independently well validated as a predictor of incremental human processing difficulty across several orders of magnitude of lexical probability (Smith and Levy, 2013). A targeted syntactic evaluation of garden-path processing can be implemented by comparing surprisal values for the disambiguating word found across the four example conditions outlined below (Futrell et al., 2019). For a model to exhibit human-like generalization, it must meet three core criteria: (i) found is less surprising (i.e., higher probability) in condition B than in condition A; (ii) found is higher probability in condition C than in condition A; (iii) the magnitude of the surprisal difference between conditions C and D is smaller than the difference between conditions A and B—a 2×2 interaction effect on surprisal that arises because the syntactic disambiguation benefit of using an unreduced relative clause is redundant when the verb is already part-of-speech unambiguous.
source_text: Although some targeted syntactic evaluations, such as the example discussed above, involve simple comparisons of conditional probabilities of a word in its context, other evaluations are more complex. We can demonstrate this with an evaluation of models' "garden-pathing" behavior (Futrell et al., 2019) . For example, the sentence The child kicked in the chaos found her way back home yields processing disruption for humans at the word found. This is because, up to right before that word, the part-of-speech ambiguous kicked is preferentially interpreted as the main verb of the sentence, whereas it turns out to be a passive participle in a reduced relative clause modifying child. This garden-path disambiguation effect is ameliorated by replacing kicked with forgotten, which is not part-of-speech ambiguous (B below; Trueswell et al., 1994) or by using an unreduced relative clause (C below; Ferreira and Clifton, 1986) . In probabilistic language models, these garden-path disambiguation effects are well captured by word negative log probabilities, or SURPRISALS (Hale, 2001) : S(w|C) = − log 2 p(w|C), which are independently well-established to predict human incremental processing difficulty over several orders of magnitude in word probability (Smith and Levy, 2013) . A targeted syntactic evaluation for gardenpathing is provided by comparing surprisals at the disambiguating word found in the set of four examples below (Futrell et al., 2019) : Successful human-like generalization involves three criteria: (i) found should be less surprising (i.e., more probable) in B than A; (ii) found should be more probable in C than A; (iii) the C-D surprisal difference should be smaller than the A-B surprisal difference-a 2 × 2 interaction effect on surprisal-because the syntactic disambiguation effect of not reducing the relative clause was achieved by using a part-of-speech unambiguous verb.

text: Additionally, we leverage SUPERT as a reward signal to train a neural reinforcement learning (RL) based summarizer, which delivers a ROUGE-2 quality improvement of up to 17% relative to state-of-the-art unsupervised summarization baselines. This result not only demonstrates the effectiveness of SUPERT when deployed in a downstream task pipeline, but also establishes a promising new paradigm for training RL-driven summarization systems: an unlimited corpus of summary-reward pairs can be generated from arbitrarily large collections of unlabeled documents, with their corresponding SUPERT scores serving as training rewards, fundamentally mitigating the data hunger limitation that confronts existing RL-based summarization frameworks.
source_text: Furthermore, we use SUPERT as rewards to train a neural-RL-based summarizer, which leads to up to 17% quality improvement (in ROUGE-2) compared to the state-of-the-art unsupervised summarizers. This result not only shows the effectiveness of SUPERT in a downstream task, but also promises a new way to train RL-based summarizers: an infinite number of summary-reward pairs can be created from infintely many documents, and their SUPERT scores can be used as rewards to train RL-based summarizers, fundamentally relieving the data-hungriness problem faced by existing RL-based summarization systems.

text: We train our proposed model using standard maximum likelihood estimation. The core optimization objective is to maximize the sum of decomposed per-step log-likelihood values, where each per-step term is computed as the sum of individual probabilities for concepts, edges, and edge labels respectively. To streamline training, we derive a reference node generation order by performing breadth-first traversal over target Abstract Meaning Representation (AMR) graphs: this traversal choice is cognitively plausible as it adheres to the core-semantic-first principle (Cai and Lam, 2019), and the efficacy of pre-order traversal is additionally empirically validated by Zhang et al. (2019a) in a depth-first traversal setting. For sibling node generation order, we initially employ a 1:1 split between uniformly random ordering and deterministic ordering sorted by relation frequency, before shifting to exclusive use of the deterministic ordering regime only during the final training steps. We empirically observe that this random-then-deterministic scheduling strategy delivers a small performance improvement.
source_text: Our model is trained with the standard maximum likelihood estimate. The optimization objective is to maximize the sum of the decomposed step-wise log-likelihood, where each is the sum of concept, edge, and edge label probabilities. To facilitate training, we create a reference generation order of nodes by running a breadth-first-traversal over target AMR graphs, as it is cognitively appealing (core-semantic-first principle, Cai and Lam, 2019) and the effectiveness of pre-order traversal is also empirically verified by Zhang et al. (2019a) in a depth-first setting. For the generation order for sibling nodes, we adopt the uniformly random order and the deterministic order sorted by the relation frequency in a 1 : 1 ratio at first then change to the deterministic order only in the final training steps. We empirically find that the deterministic-afterrandom strategy slightly improves performance.

text: Surface realization refers to the natural language generation task of producing grammatically valid, coherent sentences from structured syntactic or semantic input representations. Across multiple community shared task evaluations (Belz et al., 2011; Mille et al., 2018, 2019), the input structures provided to participating systems take the form of unordered dependency trees. The state-of-the-art system submitted to the 2019 Surface Realization Shared Task (SR’19; Yu et al., 2019a) adopts a pipeline architecture, whose first core module handles linearization: the process of assigning a sequential order to all tokens present in the input dependency tree. This framework leverages a Tree-LSTM to generate token-level encodings that incorporate global contextual awareness of the full input tree, before applying a divide-and-conquer strategy to decompose the complete tree into smaller constituent subtrees. Beam search is then deployed to identify the optimal sequential ordering for each individual subtree, and the linearized subtrees are subsequently combined to form a full projective output tree. This overarching strategy is adapted from the prior dependency parsing work introduced in Bohnet et al. (2010).
source_text: Surface realization is the task of generating a sentence from a syntactic or semantic representation. In several shared tasks (Belz et al., 2011; Mille et al., 2018 Mille et al., , 2019 , the input representations are unordered dependency trees. The state-of-the-art system (Yu et al., 2019a) in the Surface Realization Shared Task 2019 (SR'19) takes a pipeline approach, where the first step is linearization, namely ordering the tokens in the dependency tree. They use a Tree-LSTM to encode each token with awareness of the whole tree, then apply the divide-andconquer strategy to split the full tree into subtrees and find the optimal order for each subtree using beam search. Finally, the linearized subtrees are combined into a full projective tree. The general strategy is adapted from Bohnet et al. (2010) .

text: In alignment with the reporting conventions established by Martínez Alonso et al. (2016), we present cost metrics for the current annotation campaign in this section. These figures exclude both permanent staff salaries and institutional overhead costs, and are intended to serve as a proxy for the total labor required to develop a fully annotated dataset entirely from the ground up. It is important to note that while interannotator agreement scores provided early signals of overall task difficulty, they also functioned as a quantitative measure of linguistic variability across the annotation team. No two annotators on the team originated from the same subregion of North Africa, and all varied substantially in their level of prior familiarity with the subject matter discussed in the web forums selected for annotation. This variability required iterative re-annotation of sample sentences and regular updates to the annotation guidelines each time novel linguistic idiosyncrasies were identified, and crucially, formally recognized as valid by the full annotation team. Relative to the cost values reported in Martínez Alonso et al. (2016), the metrics recorded in the present study are substantially higher, at roughly five times the previously published amounts. This discrepancy stems from two key factors: first, in contrast to the aforementioned team’s work on French treebanks, no pre-existing annotation guidelines were available for our target language, and second, we were unable to retain a consistent annotation team across the full duration of the project, meaning incoming new team members required nearly full onboarding from the ground up, or were assigned to work on entirely distinct annotation layers.
source_text: Following Martínez Alonso et al. (2016), we provide here the cost figures of this annotation campaign. We do not include the salaries of the permanent staff, nor do we include the overhead. These figures are meant as an indication of the effort needed to create an annotated data set from scratch. It shall be noted that even though the interannotator agreement gave us early indications on the difficulty of the tasks, it also acted as a metric in terms of language variability among annotators. None of them come from the same part of North-Africa and none of them has the same familiarity with the topics discussed in the web-forums we annotated. We had to constantly re-annotate sentences and update the guidelines every time new idiosyncrasies were encountered and most importantly accepted as such by the annotators. Compared to what was reported in (Martínez Alonso et al., 2016) , the figures are here much higher (about 5 times higher), because unlike their work on French treebanks, we could not use preexisting guidelines for this language and because we could not keep the same team all along the project, so that new members had to be trained almost from scratch or to work on totally different layers.

text: For the machine reading comprehension task, we fine-tuned the BERT model on the SQuAD dataset within our codebase. Content transfer, a task first proposed in Prabhumoye et al. (2019), is designed to generate a sentence using knowledge sourced from an external article given a supplied input context, and we implemented this algorithm as part of the same codebase. Knowledge-grounded response generation was initially introduced in Ghazvininejad et al. (2018) and later extended as an official task in the 7th Dialog System Technology Challenge (DSTC7); for this task, we implemented the Conversation with on-demand Machine Reading (CMR) algorithm presented in Qin et al. (2019).
source_text: • Machine reading comprehension. In the codebase, we fine-tuned BERT on SQuAD. • Content transfer is a task proposed in (Prabhumoye et al., 2019) designed to, given a context, generate a sentence using knowledge from an external article. We implemented this algorithm in the codebase. • Knowledge grounded response generation is a task firstly proposed in (Ghazvininejad et al., 2018) and later extended in Dialog System Technology Challenge 7 (DSTC7) . We implemented the CMR algorithm (Conversation with on-demand Machine Reading) proposed in (Qin et al., 2019) .

text: To date, the overwhelming majority of advances in natural language understanding (NLU) research have been concentrated on English and Chinese, leaving most other languages critically underserved with respect to both publicly available pretrained models and standardized evaluation benchmarks. In this work, we present KLEJ, a comprehensive multi-task benchmark for Polish natural language understanding, modeled after the widely used English GLUE benchmark and named as an acronym for Kompleksowa Lista Ewaluacji Językowych (translated literally as Comprehensive List of Language Evaluations). KLEJ comprises nine distinct tasks, and, in alignment with the GLUE framework, is assembled primarily from preexisting publicly available Polish language datasets. We curated these tasks intentionally to span a broad diversity of text genres and capture varied core dimensions of language understanding competence. Consistent with GLUE design choices, we standardized all tasks to conform to a unified scoring framework supporting either text classification or regression outputs to streamline model evaluation workflows. In addition to the benchmark, we release HerBERT, a Transformer-based language model pretrained on multiple large Polish text corpora, and conduct systematic comparisons between HerBERT and a suite of established and state-of-the-art NLU baselines for the language.
source_text: Unfortunately, most of the progress in NLU is happening for English and Chinese. Other languages lack both pretrained models and evaluation benchmarks. In this paper, we introduce the comprehensive multi-task benchmark for the Polish language understanding -KLEJ (eng. GLUE, also abbreviation for Kompleksowa Lista Ewaluacji Językowych, eng. Comprehensive List of Language Evaluations). KLEJ consists of nine tasks and, similarly to GLUE, is constructed mostly out of existing datasets. The tasks were carefully selected to cover a wide range of genres and different aspects of language understanding. Following GLUE, to simplify a model evaluation procedure, we adjusted the tasks to fit into a unified scoring scheme (either text classification or regression). Alongside the benchmark, we introduce HerBERT, a Transformerbased model trained on several Polish text corpora. We compare HerBERT with a set of both standard and recently introduced NLU baselines.

text: Open-domain text generation models are notoriously prone to producing bland, uninformative output samples. To address this shortcoming, we implement a maximum mutual information (MMI) scoring function, as formalized in prior work (Li et al., 2016a; Zhang et al., 2018). The MMI approach leverages a pre-trained backward model to predict source sentences given corresponding target responses, or more formally, the conditional probability \(P(\text{Source}|\text{target})\). We first generate a candidate pool of response hypotheses using top-K sampling. We then use the computed conditional probability \(P(\text{Source}|\text{Hypothesis})\) to rerank all generated candidate hypotheses. Intuitively, maximizing the backward model likelihood imposes a penalty on bland hypotheses, as highly frequent, repetitive response candidates are associated with a wide range of possible source queries, and thus produce a lower conditional probability for any individual specific source query.
source_text: Open-domain text generation models are notorious for generating bland, uninformative samples. To address this problem, we implement a maximum mutual information (MMI) scoring function (Li et al., 2016a; Zhang et al., 2018) . MMI employs a pre-trained backward model to predict source sentences from given responses, i.e., P (Source|target). We first generate a set of hypotheses using top-K sampling. Then we use the probability of P (Source|Hypothesis) to rerank all hypotheses. Intuitively, maximizing backward model likelihood penalizes the bland hypotheses, as frequent and repetitive hypotheses can be associated with many possible queries, thus yielding a lower probability for any specific query.

text: Building on prior work (Li et al., 2016b; Zhang et al., 2018b), we frame dialogue generation as a sequence generation problem. Specifically, we initialize the Transmitter module using the pretrained transformer language model introduced in Radford et al. (2018) (i.e., GPT). The full training pipeline comprises two sequential steps: (1) Supervised Dialogue Generation. We optimize the Transmitter via maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) for the supervised dialogue generation task. (2) Self-Play Model Fine-Tuning. We simulate conversational exchanges between two randomly paired interlocutor agents, guiding the Transmitter to learn a policy that maximizes associated reward signals via reinforcement learning (RL) (Sutton et al., 1999). Our reward function design explicitly incorporates both standard language modeling objectives and our proposed mutual persona perception framework.
source_text: Following previous work (Li et al., 2016b; Zhang et al., 2018b) , we treat dialogue generation as a sequence generation problem. Concretely, we employ the pretraining transformer language model introduced in Radford et al. (2018) (i.e. GPT) to initialize Transmitter. The entire training procedure consists of two steps: (1) Supervised Dialogue Generation. We optimize Transmitter via maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) on the supervised dialogue generation task. (2) Self-play Model Finetuning. We simulate dialogues between two randomly paired interlocutors, encouraging Transmitter to learn a policy that maximizes reward signals via reinforcement learning (RL) (Sutton et al., 1999) . The design of the reward function considers both language modeling and our proposed mutual persona perception.

text: We implement mutual information maximization per the procedure detailed in Section 3.2. Concretely, we generate 16 candidate outputs for each input source sentence through top-K sampling (with K = 10) using a 345M-parameter model fine-tuned from the GPT-2 medium checkpoint. This sampling step is succeeded by a re-ranking stage that leverages a backward model, which is also a 345M-parameter model fine-tuned from the GPT-2 medium architecture. The candidate response that yields the lowest backward model loss is retained as the final output. Table 3 presents results from the 6K Reddit multi-reference evaluation: “Beam” denotes outputs generated via beam search, while “Human” corresponds to the held-out ground truth reference set.
source_text: We perform mutual information maximization as described in Section 3.2. Specifically, we generate 16 samples for each input source sentence by using top-K sampling (K = 10) using the 345M model fine-tuned from the GPT-2 medium model. This is followed by a re-ranking step using a backward model, which is also a 345M model finetuned from the GPT-2 medium model. The response that yields lowest backward model loss is Table 3 : 6K Reddit multi-reference evaluation. "Beam" denotes beam search. "Human" represents the held-out ground truth reference.

text: This study analyzes four datasets published in prior peer-reviewed work. Two of these corpora—the Riloff dataset (Riloff et al., 2013) and SemEval-2018 (Van Hee et al., 2018)—were annotated via a hybrid pipeline that uses distant supervision to generate an initial candidate pool, followed by manual annotation to produce final gold-standard labels. The remaining two datasets, the Ptáček corpus (Ptáček et al., 2014) and the Self-Annotated Reddit Corpus (SARC; Khodak et al., 2018), are labeled using only distant supervision. As noted in earlier sections, we recovered 1,832 valid tweets from the Riloff dataset for inclusion in our analyses. SemEval-2018 is a class-balanced corpus consisting of 4,792 total tweets. For the Ptáček dataset, we successfully retrieved 27,177 tweets from the 50,000 publicly released tweet IDs associated with the original publication. Finally, the SARC corpus comprises public Reddit comments rather than Twitter posts. Following the experimental setup of Hazarika et al. (2018), who report state-of-the-art performance on this corpus, we evaluate on two SARC variants: SARC-balanced, which contains 154,702 comments split evenly between sarcastic and non-sarcastic instances, and SARC-imbalanced, which includes 103,135 comments with a roughly 20:80 ratio of sarcastic to non-sarcastic samples.
source_text: We consider four previously published datasets. Two of them, Riloff (Riloff et al., 2013) and SemEval-2018 (Van Hee et al., 2018 , were labeled via a hybrid approach of distant supervision for initial collection and manual annotation for actual labelling. The other two datasets, Ptacek (Ptáček et al., 2014) and SARC (Khodak et al., 2018) , are labeled using distant supervision. As mentioned earlier, we managed to collect 1,832 tweets from the Riloff dataset. SemEval-2018 is a balanced dataset consisting of 4,792 tweets. For the Ptacket dataset, we collected 27,177 tweets out of the 50K published tweet IDs. Finally, The SARC datasets consists of Reddit comments. In a setting similar to Hazarika et al. (2018) who publish state-of-theart results on this dataset, we consider two variants of SARC. SARC-balanced contains 154,702 comments with the same number of sarcastic and nonsarcastic comments, while SARC-imbalanced contains 103,135 comments with a ratio of about 20:80 between sarcastic and non-sarcastic comments.

text: As outlined in Section 3, we instructed annotators to label the closest experiment-evoking word as an EXPERIMENT mention, and associate all corresponding slot arguments with that mention. Additionally, pairs of EXPERIMENT annotations could be connected via either same-experiment or experiment-variation relational links. Table 10 presents descriptive statistics for the number of EXPERIMENT annotations per sentence, as well as the frequency with which the primary annotator exercised the option to create links between distinct experiment mentions. Across the 703 experiment-describing sentences in the training data, 135 include more than one experiment-evoking word: 114 sentences contain two EXPERIMENT annotations, 18 contain three, and 3 contain four (see Table 10). Of the 114 sentences with exactly two EXPERIMENT annotations, only 2 contained mentions with no associated relational links. When presented with these unlinked cases for review, our primary annotator judged that one of the pairs should in fact have been linked.
source_text: As stated in Section 3, we instructed annotators to mark the closest experiment-evoking word as EX-PERIMENT and link the respective slot arguments to this mention. In addition, the EXPERIMENT annotations could then be linked either by same exp or exp variation links. Table 10 shows some statistics on the number of EXPERIMENT annotations per sentence and how often the primary annotator actually made use of the possibility to link experiments. In the training data, out of 703 sentences describing experiments, 135 contain more than one experiment-evoking word, with 114 sentences containing two, 18 sentences containing three, and 3 sentences containing four EXPERIMENT annotations (see Table 10 ). In the 114 sentences containing two experiment annotations, only in 2 sentences, the EXPERIMENTs were not linked to any others. Upon being shown these cases, our primary annotator judged that one of them should actually have been linked.

text: The design of our model takes inspiration from the biaffine dependency parser introduced by Dozat and Manning (2016), but differs substantially from this existing work across numerous key characteristics. Their implementation leverages a bidirectional LSTM to encode the sequential information associated with input tokens, while the biaffine attention mechanism itself does not model sequential structure. Each cell \(s_{i,j}\) in their model’s output matrix S corresponds to the assigned score for the directed dependency arc (i, j). To extract the final parse structure, they utilize a Maximal Spanning Tree algorithm to generate a tree that maximizes the total score of all arcs included in the resulting tree.
source_text: Our model is inspired by the biaffine dependency parser of Dozat and Manning (2016) , but stands in contrast in many aspects. They use a bidirectional LSTM to encode the sequential information of the tokens, and the biaffine attention itself does not model the sequence. Each cell s i,j in their output matrix S is interpreted as the score of a dependency arc (i, j). They use a Maximal Spanning Tree algorithm to obtain a tree that maximizes the total score of the arcs in the tree.

text: Quantitative Evaluation We utilize two distinct quantitative metrics to evaluate the performance of our proposed model. (1) Word-level accuracy: For a given target word, a prediction is deemed correct if and only if all diacritic marks associated with the word are fully accurate, including gemination markers and the “shin” dot, with all matres lectionis properly excluded from the output. (2) Character-level accuracy: For each individual Hebrew letter present in the input text, we validate whether the model generates the correct complete set of diacritic marks for that character; for matres lectionis specifically, we separately verify that the model correctly predicts their removal as required.
source_text: Quantitative Evaluation We use two quantitative measures to evaluate our model. (1) Word-level accuracy: for a given word 8 , we consider the prediction correct if and only if all the diacritic marks on the word are correct, including gemination and the 'shin' dot, with all matres lectionis removed. 2Character-level accuracy: For each Hebrew letter in the input text we check if the model predicted the correct set of diacritic marks for the letter (and, for matres lectionis, we check that the model predicted their removal).

text: A number of consistent observations may be drawn from the data. First, the English-French (En-Fr) and English-Russian (En-Ru) matrices are structurally comparable, with main diagonal elements comprising the vast majority of entries: these elements represent 60% of total edges in the En-Fr matrix, and 55% of total edges in the En-Ru matrix. Compounds, which are overwhelmingly noun compounds in English, constitute a notable exception to this pattern: Russian does not have a fully productive nominal compounding process, while French compounds are annotated under distinct alternative relation categories in the Universal Dependencies (UD) framework (Kahane et al., 2017). The remaining three matrices exhibit substantially lower main diagonal dominance, with main diagonal entries accounting for 46% of total alignments in the English-Chinese (En-Zh) matrix, 32% in the English-Korean (En-Ko) matrix, and 25.8% in the English-Japanese (En-Jp) matrix. These three matrices also demonstrate higher entropy across most of their rows, especially for the nmod, amod, obl, and xcomp relations, with compound again emerging as a prominent outlier. Full entropy matrices for all tested relations are available in Appendix D.
source_text: Several observations can be made. First, the En-Fr and En-Ru matrices are similar and are dominated by the elements on the main diagonal (60% of the total number of edges in En-Fr and 55% in En-Ru). An exception are compounds (which in En are mostly noun compounds), as Ru does not have a truly productive nominal compounding process and Fr compounds are annotated as other relations in UD (Kahane et al., 2017). The other three matrices are less dominated by the entries on the main diagonal (46% of the alignments in En-Zh, 32% in En-Ko, 25.8% in En-Jp) and show higher entropy in most rows, especially in nmod, amod, obl, and xcomp, compound again being a notable exception (entropy matrices for all relations can be found in Appendix D).

text: A widely adopted general approach to model introspection is the use of probing tasks. Probing tasks (Shi et al., 2016; Adi et al., 2017), also referred to as diagnostic classifiers, are a well-validated method for analyzing the presence of specific target information in a model’s latent representations, with applications spanning use cases including machine translation, language modeling (Giulianelli et al., 2018), and sentence encoding. For each probing task, a classifier is trained on a set of the model’s extracted latent representations, and its predictive performance provides a quantitative measure of how explicitly the target information is encoded. Probing tasks are typically selected to align with the model’s intended downstream task: for example, an encoder trained on relation extraction (RE) may be probed for the entity type of a given relation argument. If the classifier correctly predicts this entity type, the result implies the encoder retains entity type information in its output representations, which also directly informs downstream relation prediction. The simplicity of this approach makes it substantially easier to pinpoint the specific information a model relies on for inference, as opposed to probing the full downstream task directly.
source_text: A general approach to model introspection is the use of probing tasks. Probing tasks (Shi et al., 2016; Adi et al., 2017) , or diagnostic classifiers, are a well established method to analyze the presence of specific information in a model's latent representations, e.g. in machine-translation , language modeling (Giulianelli et al., 2018) , and sentence encoding ). For each probing task, a classifier is trained on a set of representations, and its performance measures how well the information is encoded. The probing task itself is typically selected in accordance with the downstream task, e.g. an encoder trained on RE may be probed for the entity type of a relation argument. If the classifier correctly predicts the type, it implies the encoder retains entity type information in the representations, which also directly inform the relation prediction. The simplicity of this ap-proach makes it easier to pinpoint the information a model is relying on, as opposed to probing the downstream task directly.

text: Drawing on a well-documented psycholinguistic phenomenon of syntactic priming, Prasad et al. (2019, hereafter PvSL) design an experimental paradigm to evaluate whether long short-term memory (LSTM) networks can reliably discriminate between sentences exhibiting distinct syntactic structures. When human participants are exposed to a prime input matching a particular syntactic form, they experience a transient elevation in their expectation for subsequent inputs that conform to the same structure, relative to their baseline expectations for inputs with divergent syntactic properties. This effect yields a rigorous test bed for probing questions of syntactic representation: after being primed on sentences with a given syntactic structure, subsequent sentences sharing that structure consistently elicit lower surprisal responses compared to sentences with non-matching syntactic configurations.
source_text: Prasad et al. (PvSL; 2019) leverage an analogy from psycholinguistic syntactic priming to test whether an LSTM is able to distinguish between sentences with different syntactic structures. When human subjects are primed by receiving an example of some input, their expectation of receiving similar subsequent input will temporarily increase relative to their expectation of other inputs. This can be used to test questions about syntax because once one is primed with sentences with a specific structure, subsequent sentences with shared structure will tend to show decreased surprisal responses relative to those with different structures.

text: To quantify the extent to which noise inherent in crowdsourced annotations impacts observed downstream model performance, we first conduct a targeted analysis of label quality across the TACRED dataset. We posit that while crowdsourcing workers with no specialized relation annotation training generally produce reasonably accurate labels for straightforward relation mentions—such as instances with unambiguous syntactic or lexical surface triggers, or relation types with clearly defined entity type constraints including per:title—they consistently make errors on more challenging candidate examples, including highly ambiguous mentions and relation classes whose semantic scope is not explicitly delineated in annotation documentation.
source_text: In order to identify the impact of potentially noisy, crowd-generated labels on the observed model performance, we start with an analysis of TACRED's label quality. We hypothesize that while comparatively untrained crowd workers may on average produce relatively good labels for easy relation mentions, e.g. those with obvious syntactic and/or lexical triggers, or unambiguous entity type signatures such as per:title, they may frequently err on challenging examples, e.g. highly ambiguous ones or relation types whose scope is not clearly defined.

text: In the present study, we conduct a comparative evaluation of multiple reference-free machine translation (MT) evaluation metrics rooted in cutting-edge advances in multilingual representation learning, specifically cross-lingual contextualized embeddings (Devlin et al., 2019) and cross-lingual sentence encoders (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019). We examine two distinct categories of cross-lingual reference-free metrics: (1) Soft token-level alignment metrics, which compute the optimal soft alignment between a source sentence and its corresponding system-generated translation via Word Mover’s Distance (WMD) (Kusner et al., 2015). Recent work by Zhao et al. (2019) demonstrates that WMD applied to BERT-derived representations (Devlin et al., 2019) substantially outperforms standard baseline MT evaluation metrics in reference-based evaluation settings. In this work, we investigate whether WMD can achieve comparable performance in the reference-free (i.e., cross-lingual) evaluation framework; (2) Sentence-level similarity metrics, which quantify the semantic similarity between the sentence-level representations of the source input and system translation using cosine similarity.
source_text: In this work, we comparatively evaluate a number of reference-free MT evaluation metrics that build on the most recent developments in multilingual representation learning, namely cross-lingual contextualized embeddings (Devlin et al., 2019) and cross-lingual sentence encoders (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019). We investigate two types of crosslingual reference-free metrics: (1) Soft token-level alignment metrics find the optimal soft alignment between source sentence and system translation using Word Mover's Distance (WMD) (Kusner et al., 2015) . Zhao et al. (2019) recently demonstrated that WMD operating on BERT representations (Devlin et al., 2019) substantially outperforms baseline MT evaluation metrics in the reference-based setting. In this work, we investigate whether WMD can yield comparable success in the reference-free (i.e., cross-lingual) setup; (2) Sentence-level similarity metrics measure the similarity between sentence representations of the source sentence and system translation using cosine similarity.

text: For the optimal transport (OT)-based regularizer, we employ a two-stage training workflow: we first train the model without the OT regularizer for approximately 20,000 steps, then fine-tune the network with the OT regularizer activated for an additional 10,000 steps. All models are trained using the Adam optimizer (Kingma and Ba, 2015), with hyperparameters set as follows: learning rate α = 0.00001, batch size = 4096 tokens, and second momentum parameter β₂ = 0.998. Tables 1 and 2 present experimental results across a suite of evaluation metrics, benchmarked against multiple competing baselines. The entry labeled “Ours” refers to our full proposed model, which integrates a copy mechanism, embedding factorization, noun-specific OT matching, and latent similarity loss¹. Our model outperforms all existing baseline approaches on every automatic evaluation metric, a result that demonstrates the high quality of its generated text outputs. The superior PARENT-T precision and recall scores achieved by our model further indicate that its generated outputs are more factually faithful than those produced by comparator systems. Excerpted performance values are as follows: our model records a precision of 76.3, recall of 62.1, F₁ measure of 68.02, fluency score of 2.98, and grammar score of 3.06, while the Seq2Seq baseline (Bahdanau et al., 2015) records a score of 70 on the corresponding metric. As shown in Figure 4, blue shading marks rows that appear in the input table but are omitted from the generated output text, while red shading marks entities that appear in the generated text but are not present in the input table.
source_text: For the optimal-transport based regularizer, we first train the model without OT for about 20,000 steps, then fine tune the network with OT for about 10,000 steps. We use the Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2015) optimizer to train the models. We set the hyper-parameters of Adam optimizer accordingly, including the learning rate α = 0.00001, and the two momentum parameters, batch size = 4096 (tokens) and β 2 = 0.998. Table 1 and 2 show the experiment results in terms of different evaluation metrics compared with different baselines. "Ours" means our proposed model with components of copy mechanism, embedding factorization, OT-matching with nouns, and latent similarity loss 1 . We can see that our model outperforms existing models in all of the automatic evaluation scores, indicating high quality of the generated texts. The superiority of the PARENT-T scores (in terms of precision and recall) indicates that the generated text from our model is more faithful than others. The blue color indicates the corresponding row appears in the input table, but not in the output generation text. text: Language Generation functionality is streamlined almost to its theoretical minimum in our proposed specification: each node is directly linked to a single system turn, its associated Prompt, which is generated when the node is accessed during dialogue traversal. To support more forgiving end-user interaction experiences, we include two additional optional sources of system-generated text. The first is the Reprompt, displayed in direct response to a REJECT output from the semantic parser, which permits dialogue authors to guide users toward an utterance that can be successfully parsed. The second source of system text is an optional message attached to RepeatedFail edges, intended to enable explicit acknowledgement of the potentially unexpected state transition.
source_text: Language Generation is simplified nearly to its limit in our specification; each node is directly associated with a system turn, its Prompt, produced when the node is visited. To allow for more forgiving reader experiences we add two additional optional sources of system text. The first is a Reprompt, shown in response to a REJECT from the semantic parser, allowing the author to guide the reader towards a sucessfully parseable utterance. The second source of system text is an optional message attached to RepeatedFail edges, to allow acknowledgement of the perhaps unexpected transition.

text: We formalize the conditional probability \( P(W \mid d, C_r) \) as \( P(W \mid f_s, f_a, C_r) \) under our proposed character-based model (CM): in contrast to the DGA framework, the features \( f_s \) and \( f_a \) are modeled explicitly through their raw orthographic forms, rather than via precomputed intermediate representations. This design choice gives the CM direct access to phonological information, a well-documented core predictor of morphologically complex word formation (MWF; relevant supporting background is provided in Section 2.2). While the CM may acquire implicit semantic associations over the course of training, no explicit semantic features are supplied as structured input to the architecture. Prior work has established that character-based models yield highly competitive performance across a suite of derivational morphology tasks (Deutsch et al., 2018), providing clear empirical motivation for evaluating their efficacy on the MWF prediction task explored in this work.
source_text: We model P (W |d, C r ) as P (W |f s , f a , C r ) using a character-based model (CM), i.e., as opposed to the DGA, f s and f a are modeled directly by means of their orthographic form. This provides the CM with phonological information, a central predictor of MWF (see Section 2.2). CM might also learn semantic information during training, but it is not directly provided with it. Character-based models show competitive results on derivational tasks Deutsch et al., 2018) , a good reason to test their performance on MWF prediction.
