We frame targeted syntactic evaluation tasks as structured test suites, the high-level layout of which is visualized in Figure 2. As the core foundational component of standard psycholinguistic model assessment, these test suites will be familiar to researchers with prior experience in psycholinguistic experimental design. To demonstrate the typical structure of a test suite, we use the running subject-verb agreement example introduced in the previous section. We outline test suite components in bottom-up order:
Regions
The atomic unit of a test suite is a region: a (potentially empty) string, such as the matrix verb region annotated in Figure 2. Sequences of regions can be concatenated to produce complete grammatical sentences.
source_text: We represent targeted syntactic evaluations as test suites, visualized in Figure 2 . These test suites are the core component of psycholinguistic assessment, and should be familiar to those experienced in psycholinguistic experimental design. We will present the structure of a test suite using the running example of subject-verb agreement, introduced in the previous section. We describe the components of a test suite from bottom-up: Regions The atomic unit of a test suite is a region: a (possibly empty) string, such as the matrix verb region in Figure 2 . Regions can be concatenated to form full sentences.

text: TF-IDF performs only marginally better than random guessing, a result that indicates no statistically detectable associative signal exists between dialogue context and positive response candidates. By comparison, TF-IDF yields an R@1 score of 54.98% on the Ubuntu Corpus, which demonstrates that retrieving correct responses via lexical overlap is substantially more challenging on our proposed dataset. We then benchmark the performance of standard retrieval-based dialogue models on the MuTual dataset. As shown in Table 3, carefully engineered matching models do not outperform the simple dual LSTM baseline, and further exhibit an absolute R@1 drop of over 50 points relative to their performance on the Ubuntu Corpus, indicating that text matching models are poorly suited to address reasoning-heavy dialogue tasks.
source_text: TF-IDF only slightly better than randomly guessing, which indicates that there is no obvious statistic clue between context and positive response. In contrast, TF-IDF achieves 54.98% R@1 score on the Ubuntu Corpus, showing our dataset is more difficult to get the correct answer by text overlap. We evaluate typical retrieved-based dialogue models' performance on MuTual. From Table 3 , we can see that well-designed matching models do not give better performance compared with simple dual LSTM, moreover, they drop by more than 50 absolute R@1 points compared to their performance on the Ubuntu Corpus, indicating that text matching models cannot handle reasoning problem well.

text: Indexing operations are managed by Lucene.¹⁰ We currently deploy Odinson (Valenzuela-Escárcega et al., 2020),¹¹ an open-source Lucene-based query engine developed at Lum.ai that serves as the successor to Odin (Valenzuela-Escárcega et al., 2015), which supports indexing of syntactic graphs and execution of efficient path queries across these structures. Our custom queries are converted into Odinson path queries corresponding to the longest path in our input query graph. We then iterate through all returned Odinson match results to validate any constraints that do not fall along this mapped path. At a conceptual level, the Odinson system operates by first using Lucene’s reverse index to retrieve all sentences that contain a token matching each of the specified token constraints, then validating the syntactic constraints that apply between tokens. To enhance the selectivity of Lucene queries, each token is indexed with metadata for its incoming and outgoing syntactic edge labels, which are integrated as additional token constraints for the Lucene engine. The system readily supports datasets of millions of sentences, returning query results at interactive response speeds.
source_text: The indexing is handled by Lucene. 10 We currently use Odinson (Valenzuela-Escárcega et al., 2020) , 11 an open-source Lucene-based query engine developed at Lum.ai, as a successor of Odin (Valenzuela-Escárcega et al., 2015) , that allows to index syntactic graphs and issue efficient path queries on them. We translate our queries into an Odinson path query that corresponds to a longest path in our query graph. We then iterate over the returned Odinson matches and verify the constraints that were not on the path. Conceptually, the Odinson system works by first using Lucene's reverse-index for retrieving sentences for which there is a token matching each of the specified token-constraints, and then verifying the syntactic between-token constraints. To improve the Lucene-query selectivity, tokens are indexed with incoming and outgoing syntactic edge label information, which is incorporated as additional token-constraints to the Lucene engine. The system easily supports millions of sentences, returning results at interactive speeds.

text: For the purposes of this study, we explicitly delimit the operational scope of politeness per two guiding constraints: First, we adopt the data-driven definition of politeness proposed in Danescu-Niculescu-Mizil et al. (2013). Second, our experiments are run on a custom dataset derived from the Enron corpus (Klimt and Yang, 2004), a collection of internal email exchanges from employees of a United States-based corporation. Taken together, these constraints restrict our analysis exclusively to politeness norms as broadly accepted by speakers of North American English in formal professional settings.
source_text: We circumscribe the scope of politeness for the purpose of this study as follows: First, we adopt the data driven definition of politeness proposed by (Danescu-Niculescu-Mizil et al., 2013) . Second, we base our experiments on a dataset derived from the Enron corpus (Klimt and Yang, 2004) which consists of email exchanges in an American corporation. Thus, we restrict our attention to the notion of politeness as widely accepted by the speakers of North American English in a formal setting.

text: We formalize the content selection task to steer models toward identifying semantically relevant content and constructing logically sound reasoning chains over structured semantic graphs. To conduct quantitative validation of relevant content selection performance, we compute the alignment of model-generated node attention weights, as illustrated via the following reference entries: 2) The phrase “Na Na” appears in the Disney feature film *Confessions of a Teenage Drama Queen*. 3) *Confessions of a Teenage Drama Queen* is a 2004 American teen musical comedy film directed by Sara Sugarman, and produced by Robert Shapiro and Matthew Hart for Walt Disney Pictures.
source_text: We introduced the content selection task to guide the model to select relevant content and form proper reasoning chains in the semantic graph. To quantitatively validate the relevant content selection, we calculate the alignment of node attention 2) " Na Na " appeared on the Disney film , " Confessions of a Teenage Drama Queen " . 3) Confessions of a Teenage Drama Queen is a 2004 American teen musical comedy film directed by Sara Sugarman and produced by Robert Shapiro and Matthew Hart for Walt Disney Pictures .

text: Existing semantically grounded metrics for reference-free machine translation (MT) system evaluation have thus far exhibited consistently weak correlation with human judgments of translation quality. In the present work, we examine a suite of reference-free metrics built on state-of-the-art models for deriving cross-lingual semantic representations: cross-lingual contextualized word embeddings and cross-lingual sentence embeddings. We identify key failure modes for these metrics, most notably their failure to penalize literal, word-for-word translations, a phenomenon widely referred to as “translationese.” We test two distinct mechanisms to mitigate this undesirable limitation: (1) an auxiliary weakly supervised cross-lingual alignment step that reduces representational mismatch between pairs of mutual translations, and (2) target-side language modeling (LM), which is natively suited to penalizing unnatural phrasing and ungrammatical sentences in the target language. We demonstrate that a reference-free metric combining cross-lingual similarity scores with target-side LM scoring outperforms the reference-based BLEU metric on segment-level MT evaluation tasks. Our findings carry two notable implications. First, they confirm the feasibility of reference-free MT evaluation and justify expanded research investment in this direction. Second, they indicate that reference-free MT evaluation represents a uniquely challenging “adversarial” evaluation task for multilingual text encoders, as it exposes critical limitations that remain hidden in these encoders’ standard evaluation scenarios—most notably, an inability to detect semantically incoherent word-for-word translations or invalid paraphrases.
source_text: Existing semantically-motivated metrics for reference-free evaluation of MT systems have so far displayed rather poor correlation with human estimates of translation quality. In this work, we investigate a range of reference-free metrics based on cutting-edge models for inducing cross-lingual semantic representations: cross-lingual (contextualized) word embeddings and cross-lingual sentence embeddings. We have identified some scenarios in which these metrics fail, prominently their inability to punish literal word-by-word translations (the so-called "translationese"). We have investigated two different mechanisms for mitigating this undesired phenomenon: (1) an additional (weakly-supervised) cross-lingual alignment step, reducing the mismatch between representations of mutual translations, and 2language modeling (LM) on the target side, which is inherently equipped to punish "unnatural" sentences in the target language. We show that the reference-free coupling of cross-lingual similarity scores with the target-side language model surpasses the reference-based BLEU in segment-level MT evaluation. We believe our results have two relevant implications. First, they portray the viability of referencefree MT evaluation and warrant wider research efforts in this direction. Second, they indicate that reference-free MT evaluation may be the most challenging ("adversarial") evaluation task for multilingual text encoders as it uncovers some of their shortcomings-prominently, the inability to capture semantically non-sensical word-by-word translations or paraphrases-which remain hidden in their common evaluation scenarios.

text: For all model training procedures, we source data from the 4th version of the ParaCrawl parallel corpus (Esplà-Gomis et al., 2019). Collected via broad public web crawling, this corpus contains noisy segment pairs that are known to negatively impact machine translation model performance. We work exclusively with the subset of the corpus pre-filtered via version 5 of the bicleaner cleaning tool (Sánchez-Cartagena et al., 2018), which contains 2,156,069 parallel segment pairs ranked by their assigned bicleaner quality score; this predefined ranking enables direct performance comparison between the existing bicleaner pipeline and our proposed cleaning toolkit. We first generate six baseline training splits: the full untruncated filtered dataset, plus five reduced subsets created by removing the lowest-quality 10%, 20%, 30%, 40%, and 50% of pairs from the noisy tail of the bicleaner-ranked collection, and train separate translation models on each of these six splits. We then re-sort the full filtered segment pair collection using the quality ranking output by our toolkit, and construct a parallel set of training splits by truncating low-scoring pairs at the exact same proportional cutoffs used for the baseline experiments.
source_text: For training, we use data from version 4 of the ParaCrawl corpus (Esplà-Gomis et al., 2019). The data is taken from a general internet crawl and contains segments that are noisy and potentially harmful for machine translation models. We use the subset of the corpus that is already filtered by the bicleaner tool 5 (Sánchez-Cartagena et al., 2018) . This data set contains 2,156,069 segment pairs and is ordered by the score from bicleaner, which enables us to directly compare it to our tool. We create five versions of the training data by removing 10%, 20%, 30%, 40% and 50% of the pairs from the noisy end of the collection and train translation models with the full data and with the five reduced training sets. Next, we reorder the data with our toolkit and again create new data sets by removing data with the same proportions as previously.

text: Finally, we conduct a correlation analysis examining the relationship between translation difficulty as measured by XMI and the linguistic and data-dependent properties of each translation task, following the approaches of  and . Table 2 reports Pearson and Spearman correlation coefficients for all data-dependent metrics, with bolded values indicating statistically significant results (p < 0.05) after Bonferroni correction for multiple comparisons (adjusted threshold p < 0.0029). Notably, the only features that exhibit a significant correlation with our difficulty metric are source language type-to-token ratio (TTR) and the pairwise distance between source and target language TTR values. This finding implies that lexical variation offers a potential explanatory mechanism for observed cross-task differences in translation difficulty. Full correlation results are provided in Appendix D.
source_text: Last, we conduct a correlation study between the translation difficulties as measured by XMI and the linguistic and data-dependent properties of each translation task, following the approaches of and . Table 2 lists Pearson's and Spearman's correlation coefficients for data-dependent metrics, where bold values indicate statistically significant results (p < 0.05) after Bonferroni correction (p < 0.0029). Interestingly, the only features that significantly correlate with our metric are related to the type-to-token ratio (TTR) for the source language and the distance between source and target TTRs. This implies that a potential explanation for the differences in translation difficulty lies in lexical variation. For full correlation results, refer to App. D.

text: We randomly sampled 400 dialogue pairs for expert annotation. Annotators were prompted with the question: “If you were the end user, in which scenario would you be more likely to return to interact with the system again?” We instructed experts to prioritize overall user experience in their judgments, rather than evaluating the performance of any individual module of the dialogue system. Annotation was completed by two researchers with formal training in conversational system evaluation; the senior annotator had over one year of experience working on an Alexa competition team, with a specialized focus on user rating protocols. For each dialogue pair (A, B), annotators assigned one of three labels: “A is better than B”, “B is better than A”, or “cannot tell”. Inter-annotator agreement was high, with a Cohen’s kappa (Cohen, 1968) of κ = 0.83. To ensure the accuracy of development and test set annotations, all pairs labeled “cannot tell” were excluded from further analysis. We next examined the correlation between Likert-scale based evaluation and pairwise comparison based evaluation. To further investigate self-reported Likert scale ratings, we compared the expert annotation labels for the 403 total dialogue pairs against the corresponding self-reported Likert scale ratings for these interactions. For each dialogue pair, we aligned the pairwise comparison label with the delta between the two dialogues’ self-reported Likert scale ratings. Under ideal alignment, the dialogue with the higher self-reported Likert rating would be the same dialogue annotated as offering better user experience in the pairwise comparison. We calculated the count and proportion of “disagreement” cases where the two rating types produced conflicting results. Overall, disagreement was observed for approximately one-third of all dialogue pairs. As shown in Table 2, the rate of disagreement between expert annotations and self-reported ratings decreases as the magnitude of the gap between the two dialogues’ self-reported Likert scale ratings grows. This finding indicates that when the difference between two dialogues’ Likert scores is substantial, the two evaluation frameworks are far more likely to produce consistent results.
source_text: We randomly sample 400 dialog pairs for experts to annotate. We ask the question, "If you were the user, in which scenario would you be more likely to come back and talk to the system again? " We guide the experts to focus on the user experience rather than calibrating the performance of any specific module of the dialog system. Two researchers with conversational training experience annotated the data. The leading expert has been working in an Alexa competition team for more than one year with an emphasis on the user ratings. For each dialog pair (A, B), they label 'A is better than B' or 'B is better than A' or 'cannot tell'. They reached a high inter-annotator agreement score (Cohen, 1968) with kappa κ = 0.83. To make sure that the dev & test is accurate, we throw away all "cannot tell" dialog pairs. We then study the correlation between Likert score based evaluation and pairwise comparison based evaluation. To further analyze the self-reported Likert scale ratings, we also compare the annotated labels of the 403 dialog pairs with the self-reported Likert scale ratings of these dialogs. For each pair of dialogs, we compare the pairwise comparison label and the delta between the self-reported Likert scale ratings of the two dialogs. Ideally, the dialog with a higher self-reported Likert scale rating should be the one that is annotated as having a better user experience in the pairwise comparison. We count the number and fraction of "disagreement" between the two types of ratings. Overall, roughly 1/3 of the dialog pairs disagree. As shown in Table 2 , as the gap between the self-reported Likert scale ratings becomes larger, the disagreement between expert and self-reported ratings goes down. This suggests that if the difference between the two dialogs' Likert score is huge, they are more likely to be consistent with the comparison ratings.

text: Reordered source sentences are generated via the BTG-based preordering model proposed in Neubig et al. (2012), which is trained on the previously outlined sub-word level 1 parallel corpus. During the training phase, we first derive word alignments from the parallel dataset using either GIZA++ or FastAlign, before proceeding to the core training step of identifying the optimal BTG tree for each source sentence that is consistent with the corresponding target sentence word order, using the computed word alignments and parallel dataset as inputs. For the decoding phase, only source sentences are provided as input to the trained model, which produces reordering indices that are then passed to the downstream neural machine translation (NMT) model. Accordingly, bilingual alignment information is only utilized for preprocessing training data, and is not required at the decoding stage.
source_text: The reordered source sentences are generated by BTG-based preordering model (Neubig et al., 2012) trained with above sub-word level 1 parallel corpus. At training phase, we first obtain word alignments from parallel data using GIZA++ or FastAlign, and then the training process is to find the optimal BTG tree for source sentence consistent with the order of the target sentence based on the word alignments and parallel data. At decoding phase, we only provide source sentences as input and the model can output reordering indices, which will be fed into NMT model. Thus, bilingual alignment information is only used to preprocess training data, but not necessary at decoding time.

text: Concurrently, the functional properties of individual attention heads in Transformer architectures have garnered significant targeted research attention. Clark et al. (2019) demonstrated that standalone heads can identify standard part-of-speech (POS) tags and dependency (DEP) relations, including prepositional objects (POBJ) and determiners (DET), with high fidelity to human-annotated labels. Vig and Belinkov (2019) further explored cross-head dependency encoding patterns across model depth, finding that early layers predominantly represent positional relational information, middle layers capture the vast majority of dependency relations, and upper layers specialize in detecting unique linguistic patterns and structural properties. These empirical insights are made available for interactive exploration through the EXBERT tool.
source_text: In parallel, individual attention heads of Transformer models have also received much focus. Clark et al. (2019) showed that individual heads recognize standard Part of Speech (POS) and Dependency (DEP) relationships (e.g., Objects of the Preposition (POBJ) and Determinants (DET)) with high fidelity. Vig and Belinkov (2019) also explored the dependency relations across heads and discovered that initial layers typically encode positional relations, middle layers capture the most dependency relations, and later layers look for unique patterns and structures. These insights are exposed interactively through EXBERT.

text: Overview We model German nominal number inflection using the sequence-to-sequence encoder-decoder architecture introduced in Sutskever et al. (2014). This framework comprises two recurrent neural network (RNN) components: one RNN reads the full input sequence and encodes it into a fixed-length vector representation, while a second RNN incrementally decodes this representation to generate the target output sequence. Following the method established in Kann and Schütze (2016), our decoder incorporates the neural attention mechanism proposed in Bahdanau et al. (2015). For our morphological transduction task, the encoder-decoder (ED) model takes character-level representations of singular German nouns as inputs (e.g. m H U N D eos), and is trained to produce the corresponding inflected plural form of the noun (e.g. H U N D E eos). Every input character sequence is prefixed with m, f, or n to indicate the noun’s grammatical gender. Unlike English, German has a highly transparent phonology-to-orthography mapping, so we are able to use written corpus data exclusively for model training. We reserve a held-out development set for hyperparameter selection, and a separate held-out test set to assess the model’s generalization accuracy for unseen German nouns. In addition, the 24 M95 nouns, which we use to compare model outputs to human speaker behavioral patterns, are presented to the model marked as neuter gender, consistent with the protocol used in Study 1.
source_text: Overview We model German number inflection using the sequence-to-sequence Encoder-Decoder architecture (Sutskever et al., 2014) . This comprises a recurrent neural network (RNN) which reads in an input sequence and encodes it into a fixed-length vector representation, and another RNN which incrementally decodes that representation into an output sequence. Following Kann and Schütze (2016) , our decoder uses neural attention (Bahdanau et al., 2015) . For our task of morphological transduction, the ED model takes character-level representations of German nouns in their singular form as inputs (e.g. m H U N D eos ), and learns to produce the noun's inflected plural form (e.g. H U N D E eos ). Each character sequence starts with m , f , or n , to indicate grammatical gender. Unlike English, the phonological-orthographic mapping is straightforward in German, so we can use a written corpus for model training. We keep a held-out dev set for hyperparameter selection, and a held-out test set to asses the model's accuracy in generalizing to unseen German nouns. In addition, the 24 M95 nouns were used for comparison with speaker behavior. They were presented to the model as neuter gender, consistent with Study 1.

text: Two standard, widely adopted ad-hoc retrieval models are used to rank documents relative to input queries: BM25 and query likelihood (QL), both of which are implemented in the Anserini information retrieval (IR) toolkit. These models leverage unsupervised term-weighting techniques calculated from corpus-wide statistics, so the addition of keyphrases to a given document will produce direct, straightforward impacts on their output. We further implement the RM3 pseudorelevance feedback method (Abdul-Jaleel et al., 2004) layered on top of these base models to achieve strong, near state-of-the-art retrieval results (Lin, 2019; . Default parameter settings provided with the Anserini toolkit are applied for all included models.
source_text: We consider two standard ad-hoc retrieval models to rank documents against queries: BM25 and query likelihood (QL), both implemented in the Anserini IR toolkit . These models use unsupervised techniques based on corpus statistics for term weighting, and will therefore be straightforwardly affected when keyphrases are added to a document. We further apply a pseudorelevance feedback method, known as RM3 (Abdul-Jaleel et al., 2004) , on top of the models to achieve strong, near state-of-the-art retrieval results (Lin, 2019; . For all models, we use Anserini's default parameters.

text: For the English-language dataset, we first translate all constituent texts into each of the four target languages using three distinct machine translation systems. We then again predict author demographic attributes both for the held-out original English test set, using the classifier trained on English text, and for each translated iteration of the test set, using the classifier trained on the corresponding target language. As an illustrative example, we generate separate German, French, Italian, and Dutch translations of each English text via Google Translate, Bing Translator, and DeepL, before running classification on both the source English texts and all resulting translated outputs.
source_text: For the English data, we first translate the texts into each of the other languages, using each of the three translation systems. Then we again predict the author demographics in the original English test set (using the classifier trained on English), as well as in each of the translated versions (using the classifier trained on the respective language). E.g., we create a German, French, Italian, and Dutch translation with each Google, Bing, and DeepL, and classify both the original English and the translation.

text: By contrast, when using the Key News Themes (NSTM) framework, the same analyst can run a search for ‘Amazon.com’ across a specified time horizon and immediately obtain a concise, holistic overview of relevant news coverage, as depicted in Fig. 1. We address the core challenges associated with processing large volumes of unstructured news content by deploying state-of-the-art techniques to semantically cluster news stories, alongside innovative summarization methodologies designed to extract succinct, high-information summaries for each resulting cluster. A small set of representative key stories is then selected from each individual cluster. We formally define the triple of (story cluster, corresponding summary, associated key stories) as a single theme, while a sequentially ordered list of these themes constitutes the full overview.
source_text: By contrast, using NSTM (Key News Themes), this same analyst can search for 'Amazon.com', over a given time horizon, and promptly receive a concise and comprehensive overview of the news, as shown in Fig. 1 . We tackle the challenges involved with consuming vast quantities of news by leveraging modern techniques to semantically cluster stories, as well as innovative summarization methods to extract succinct, informational summaries for each cluster. A handful of key stories are then selected from each cluster. We define a (story cluster, summary, key stories) triple as one theme and an ordered list of themes as an overview.

text: Relation extraction (RE) refers to the task of identifying relational connections between entities referenced in textual data, where these relations map to predefined semantic categories including org:founded by, person:spouse, and org:subsidiaries (Figure 1). Neural architectures for this task have yielded remarkable performance, reaching state-of-the-art results across standard benchmark datasets such as SemEval2010 Task 8 (dos Santos et al., 2015; Lee et al., 2019), TA-CRED (Zhang et al., 2018; Alt et al., 2019b; Peters et al., 2019;, and NYT (Lin et al., 2016; Vashishth et al., 2018; Alt et al., 2019a). The majority of existing RE models employ an encoder architecture to learn a fixed-dimensional representation of the input, for example a single sentence, which is then fed to a classification layer to predict the target relation label. These strong performance outcomes indicate that the learned representations capture linguistic and semantic properties of the input that are relevant to the downstream RE task, an intuition that was previously discussed for a variety of other NLP tasks by . Despite this progress, the exact specific properties that various RE models have learned are often unknown. The aim of our work is to pinpoint the precise information a given RE model relies on to generate its predictions, both to improve overall model performance and to facilitate targeted error diagnosis.
source_text: Relation extraction (RE) is concerned with extracting relationships between entities mentioned in text, where relations correspond to semantic categories such as org:founded by, person:spouse, or org:subsidiaries (Figure 1 ). Neural models have shown impressive results on this task, achieving state-of-the-art performance on standard datasets like SemEval2010 Task 8 (dos Santos et al., 2015; Lee et al., 2019) , TA-CRED (Zhang et al., 2018; Alt et al., 2019b; Peters et al., 2019; , and NYT (Lin et al., 2016; Vashishth et al., 2018; Alt et al., 2019a) . The majority of models implement an encoder architec- ture to learn a fixed size representation of the input, e.g. a sentence, which is passed to a classification layer to predict the target relation label. These good results suggest that the learned representations capture linguistic and semantic properties of the input that are relevant to the downstream RE task, an intuition that was previously discussed for a variety of other NLP tasks by . However, it is often unknown which exact properties the various models have learned. Our aim is to pinpoint the information a given RE model is relying on, in order to improve model performance as well as to diagnose errors.

text: The SUGILITE framework adopts an approach that prompts end users to verbally articulate the underlying intent behind their demonstrated actions via spoken input. Results from our earlier formative study (Li et al., 2018a) confirm that end users are consistently able to produce useful, generalizable explanations for the intents driving the interaction sequences they demonstrate. Analysis of collected user utterances further reveals that speakers frequently leverage two distinct reference patterns for interface elements: semantic references to underlying GUI content, such as referring to an entry displaying the text “596 ft” as “the close by restaurant”, and implicit spatial references to element positioning, such as describing a numeric text object that is right-aligned adjacent to the text element “Lakers” as “the score for Lakers.”
source_text: SUGILITE's approach is to ask users to verbally explain their intent for the demonstrated actions using speech. Our formative study (Li et al., 2018a) found that end users were able to provide useful and generalizable explanations for the intents of their demonstrated actions. They also commonly used in their utterances semantic references to GUI contents (e.g., "the close by restaurant" for an entry showing the text "596 ft") and implicit spatial references (e.g., "the score for Lakers" for a text object that contains a numeric value and is right-aligned to another text object "Lakers").

text: The “Entail” column reports entailment ratio values computed using the deployed textual entailment classifier. Replicating trends observed across other performance metrics, the filtering+pseudo strategy yields the highest entailment ratio on both the Gigaword benchmark and JAMUL dataset. While this result may appear intuitively predictable given that training instance selection leveraged the same entailment classifier, the finding nonetheless highlights a valuable capability: we can adjust output entailment ratios without implementing any modifications to the core generation model. To investigate whether the filtering strategy delivers perceptible quality improvements for human users, we recruited a human annotator to evaluate the factuality of headlines generated under the baseline configuration and the filtering+pseudo strategy. For each sample, the annotator was presented with both the full source document and the corresponding model-generated headline, and asked to categorize the headline as truthful, untruthful, or incomprehensible. This evaluation covers 109 total instances randomly sampled from the held-out test sets of Gigaword and JAMUL.
source_text: The column "Entail" shows the entailment ratio measured by the entailment classifier. Again, the filtering+pseudo strategy obtained the highest entailment ratio on both the Gigaword dataset and JAMUL. Although this result may be interpreted as natural because we selected training instances based on the same entailment classifier, it is interesting to see that we can control the entailment ratio without changing the model. In order to examine whether the filtering strategy can deliver noticeable improvements for human readers, we asked a human subject to judge the truthfulness of the headlines generated by the baseline setting and filtering+pseudo strategy. Presented with both a source document and a headline generated by the model, the human subject judged whether the headline was truthful, untruthful, or incomprehensible. We conduct this evaluation for 109 instances randomly sampled from the test sets of Gigaword and JAMUL.

text: Existing extractive multi-document summarization (MDS) models typically fall into two dominant design paradigms: the first ranks candidate sentences by their estimated semantic and pragmatic importance (Hong and Nenkova, 2014; Cao et al., 2015; Yasunaga et al., 2017), while the second frames summary generation as a global optimization problem to identify high-performing sentence combinations, guided by heuristic functions that operationalize desired summary quality attributes (Gillick and Favre, 2009; Lin and Bilmes, 2011; Peyrard and Eckle-Kohler, 2016). A broad range of abstractive MDS approaches build upon foundations of multi-sentence compression and sentence fusion to produce novel, condensed output text (Ganesan et al., 2010; Banerjee et al., 2015; Chali et al., 2017; Nayeem et al., 2018). In recent work, neural sequence-to-sequence models, which represent the state of the art for abstractive single-document summarization (Rush et al., 2015; Nallapati et al., 2016; See et al., 2017), have been extended to the MDS task: prominent adaptation pipelines include applying these models to outputs of pre-existing extractive summarization systems (Liu et al., 2018), or modifying their encoding layers to directly process inputs drawn from multiple source documents (Zhang et al., 2018; Fabbri et al., 2019).
source_text: Extractive MDS models commonly focus on either ranking sentences by importance (Hong and Nenkova, 2014; Cao et al., 2015; Yasunaga et al., 2017) or on global optimization to find good combinations of sentences, using heuristic functions of summary quality (Gillick and Favre, 2009; Lin and Bilmes, 2011; Peyrard and Eckle-Kohler, 2016) . Several abstractive approaches for MDS are based on multi-sentence compression and sentence fusion (Ganesan et al., 2010; Banerjee et al., 2015; Chali et al., 2017; Nayeem et al., 2018) . Recently, neural sequence-to-sequence models, which are the state-of-the-art for abstractive single-document summarization (Rush et al., 2015; Nallapati et al., 2016; See et al., 2017) , have been used for MDS, e.g., by applying them to extractive summaries (Liu et al., 2018) or by directly encoding multiple documents (Zhang et al., 2018; Fabbri et al., 2019) .

text: For an autoregressive language model, which computes a conditional probability distribution over candidate next tokens given all preceding tokens in a sequence, a decoding strategy governs text generation by specifying a rule for selecting one output token at each timestep using the model’s predicted per-step probability distributions. One of the most conceptually straightforward decoding strategies is unconstrained random sampling, where each token is selected stochastically with probability directly proportional to its model-assigned likelihood. A core limitation of this random sampling approach stems from the heavy-tailed structure of standard token probability distributions: these distributions typically feature a long tail of vocabulary entries that have very low individual probability but collectively account for a substantial share of total probability mass. As Holtzman et al. (2020) observe, selecting tokens from this low-probability tail frequently results in semantically incoherent generated output.
source_text: Given an autoregressive language model that defines a probability distribution over the next token given the previous tokens in a sequence, a decoding strategy generates text by deciding how to output a token at each step based on the predicted distributions. Perhaps the most straightforward decoding strategy is to randomly choose a token with probability proportional to its likelihood. A challenge with the random sampling approach is that these probability distributions often contain a long tail of vocabulary items that are individually low-probability but cumulatively comprise a substantial amount of probability mass. Holtzman et al. (2020) observe that choosing tokens from this tail often leads to incoherent generations.

text: In this paper, we demonstrate that existing headline generation models produce unexpected terms. We hypothesize that one core contributing factor to this issue is flaws in both the task setting and associated datasets, specifically scenarios where source document information is sufficiently incomplete that generating a valid corresponding headline is impossible. We present an approach to filter out headlines that are not entailed by their paired source documents from the supervised training data. Experimental results confirm that headline generation models trained on this filtered supervision data show no clear difference in ROUGE scores, but exhibit remarkable improvements in both automatic and manual evaluations of the factual truthfulness of generated headlines. Our work also underscores the importance of explicitly evaluating truthfulness as a core metric for abstractive summarization tasks.
source_text: In this paper, we showed that the current headline generation model yields unexpected words. We conjectured that one of the reasons lies in the defect in the task setting and data set, where generating a headline from the source document is impossible because of the insufficiency of the source information. We presented an approach for removing from the supervision data headlines that are not entailed by their source documents. Experimental results demonstrated that the headline generation model trained on filtered supervision data showed no clear difference in ROUGE scores but remarkable im-provements in automatic and manual evaluations of the truthfulness of the generated headlines. We also presented the importance of evaluating truthfulness in abstractive summarization.

text: To confirm the efficacy of our implemented systems, we performed an intrinsic evaluation that quantified the number of exact matches between model-predicted keyphrases and ground-truth gold keyphrases. We employ the standard evaluation metric, calculating f-measure for the top 5 ranked predictions, as this threshold aligns with the average number of keyphrases documented in the KP20k and NTCIR-2 datasets, which are 5.3 and 4.8 respectively. We further assess cross-domain generalization performance using the KPTimes news dataset (Gallina et al., 2019), and incorporate a state-of-the-art unsupervised keyphrase extraction model (Boudin, 2018, referred to hereafter as mp-rank) for comparative purposes. This latter baseline additionally generates a supplementary relevance signal derived from graph-based ranking, the utility of which for retrieval tasks will be evaluated in subsequent experiments. Full results are presented in Table 2. Overall, our findings align with those reported in prior work (Meng et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2018), demonstrating the performance superiority of well-trained neural models over unsupervised counterparts, while also emphasizing the limited cross-domain robustness of these neural architectures. Somewhat unexpectedly, the seq2seq+corr model is outperformed by the seq2seq+copy architecture, a result that indicates relevant, yet potentially redundant, keyphrases are filtered out by the additional mechanisms built into seq2seq+corr to promote output diversity.
source_text: To validate the effectiveness of our implementations, we conducted an intrinsic evaluation by counting the number of exact matches between predicted and gold keyphrases. We adopt the standard metric and compute the f-measure at top 5, as it corresponds to the average number of keyphrases in KP20k and NTCIR-2, that is, 5.3 and 4.8, respectively. We also examine cross-domain generalization using the KPTimes news dataset (Gallina et al., 2019) , and include a state-of-the-art unsupervised keyphrase extraction model (Boudin, 2018 , henceforth mp-rank) for comparison purposes. This latter baseline also provides an additional relevance signal based on graph-based ranking whose usefulness in retrieval will be tested in subsequent experiments. Results are reported in Table 2 . Overall, our results are consistent with those reported in (Meng et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2018) , demonstrating the superiority of well-trained neural models over unsupervised ones, and stressing their lack of robustness across domains. Rather surprisingly, seq2seq+corr is outperformed by seq2seq+copy which indicates that relevant, yet possibly redundant, keyphrases are filtered out by the added mechanisms for promoting diversity in the output.

text: A pervasive bottleneck for task-oriented dialogue research stems from two core annotation-related limitations: not only is upfront data procurement prohibitively costly, but producing accurate dialogue annotations has proven uniquely challenging, with common pitfalls including annotator error, pipeline delays, inconsistent labeling conventions, and cross-utterance normalization issues (Zhou and Small, 2019). Figure 1 provides an example of a dialogue synthesized using our template framework, where 'U:' marks user utterances, 'A:' marks agent utterances, and 'S:' indicates the dialogue state recorded at each conversational turn. Even widely adopted benchmark datasets such as MultiWOZ exhibit notable annotation inconsistencies, despite being constructed through multiple iterative rounds of expert annotation (Budzianowski et al., 2018; Zhou and Small, 2019).
source_text: Unfortunately, not only is the initial acquisition expensive, annotating dialogues correctly has proven to be challenging due to human errors, delays in annotation, inconsistent conventions, and normalization issues Figure 1: An example of a dialogue that can be synthesized from our templates. 'U:' indicates the user, 'A:' the agent, and 'S:' is the dialogue state at each turn. Small, 2019) . The MultiWOZ dataset still has significant inconsistencies (Zhou and Small, 2019) despite having been constructed through multiple rounds of annotations (Budzianowski et al., 2018; .

text: This section introduces the extended iteration of our sensitivity study first presented in §4.3. Figure 8 compiles results for the synthetic data distribution across varying test-time and training-time noise levels (denoted η_test and η_train, respectively) and weighting factor values α. Our observations align with the consistent trend identified in our initial analysis. As test-time noise level η_test rises, overall model accuracy declines, though this downward trend is far less pronounced for configurations where α = 0. Concurrently, the performance gap between models trained with our auxiliary objectives and those trained without these objectives grows wider.
source_text: In this section, we present the extended version of our sensitivity study ( §4.3). Figure 8 summarizes the results on the synthetic data distribution with various test-and training-time noise levels (η test and η train , respectively) and weighting factors α. We noticed a similar trend as in our initial analysis. As the level of noise η test increases, the overall accuracy decreases, but this trend is less pronounced for α = 0. At the same time, the gap between the models trained with and without our auxiliary objectives becomes larger.

text: The English Gigaword corpus (Graff and Cieri, 2003; Napoles et al., 2012) is a representative benchmark dataset for abstractive summarization research. Rush et al. (2015) framed Gigaword as a corpus comprising an extensive collection of article-headline pairs suited for training encoder-decoder models. Their work adopted a task setup in which the first sentence of a given article functions as the source text, while its associated headline acts as the target text (the reference summary). Following this publication, it has become standard practice to utilize the Gigaword dataset under this specific task configuration, and to quantify the quality of model-generated headlines using ROUGE scores (Lin and Hovy, 2003) calculated between system-produced outputs and corresponding reference headlines.
source_text: English Gigaword (Graff and Cieri, 2003; Napoles et al., 2012 ) is a representative dataset for abstractive summarization. Rush et al. (2015) regarded Gigaword as a corpus containing a large number of article-headline pairs for training an encoder-decoder model. Their work assumed a task setting where the first sentence of an article is a source text and its corresponding headline is a target text (summary). Since then, it has been a common practice to use the Gigaword dataset with this task setting and to measure the quality of generated headlines with ROUGE scores (Lin and Hovy, 2003) between system-generated and reference headlines.

text: TargetMT is subsequently deployed as a distant supervision signal to train a model that generates word-level emotion predictions from target-language word embeddings. The TargetMT-train and TargetMT-dev splits are used for model parameter fitting and hyperparameter optimization respectively, while the TargetMT-test partition is reserved for held-out downstream evaluation. Once training is finalized, the model is applied to generate new emotion labels for all terms included in TargetMT, producing a second target-side emotion lexicon designated TargetPred. Our rationale for this step rests on the assumption that a sufficiently well-trained model will exhibit strong generalizability. Figure 1 presents a schematic overview of the full methodology for generating and evaluating an emotion lexicon for a specified target language using source-language supervision. The diagram includes a toy example that initiates with an English Valence-Arousal (VA) lexicon containing the terms sunshine, nuclear, and terrorism alongside their associated numerical Valence and Arousal scores, and yields an expanded German lexicon that incorporates both translated entries with adjusted VA scores and additional entries derived from the embedding model with newly inferred scores.
source_text: TargetMT is then used as the distant supervisor to train a model that predicts word emotions based on target-side word embeddings. TargetMT-train and TargetMT-dev are used to fit model parameters and optimize hyperparameters, respectively, whereas TargetMT-test is held out for later evaluation. Once finalized, the model is used to predict new labels for the words in TargetMT, resulting in a second target-side emotion lexicon denoted TargetPred. Our rationale for doing so is that a reasonably trained model should generalize well Figure 1 : Schematic view on the methodology for generating and evaluating an emotion lexicon for a given target language based on source language supervision. Included is a toy example starting with an English VA lexicon (sunshine, nuclear, terrorism and the associated numerical scores for Valence and Arousal) and resulting in an extended German lexicon which incorporates translated entries with altered VA scores and additional entries originating from the embedding model with newly learned scores.

text: Across the test dataset, the convolutional transformer (convtransformer) consistently outperforms the character-level transformer, yielding a performance gap of up to 2.3 BLEU points on the Chinese-to-English (ZH→EN) bilingual translation task, and up to 2.6 BLEU points on the French-Chinese-to-English (FR+ZH→EN) multilingual translation task. Training multilingual translation models on typologically similar source languages, as in the French-Spanish-to-English (FR+ES→EN) task, delivers performance gains for both source languages, a finding that aligns with prior work reported in Lee et al. (2017). Counterintuitively, training on typologically distant source languages remains effective under select conditions. For instance, models trained on the FR+ZH→EN multilingual task outperform single-source bilingual models trained exclusively on FR→EN, though they underperform relative to bilingual models trained only on the ZH→EN task. These results indicate that distant-language multilingual training primarily confers benefits when the source language of interest is typologically closer to the target translation language, which in this work is English.
source_text: The convtransformer consistently outperforms the character-level transformer on this dataset, with a gap of up to 2.3 BLEU on bilingual translation (ZH→EN) and up to 2.6 BLEU on multilingual translation (FR+ZH→EN). Training multilingual models on similar input languages (FR + ES→EN) leads to improved performance for both languages, which is consistent with (Lee et al., 2017) . Training on distant languages is surprisingly still effective in some cases. For example, the models trained on FR+ZH→EN outperform the models trained just on FR→EN; however they perform worse than the bilingual models trained on ZH→EN. Thus, distant-language training seems to be helpful mainly when the input language is closer to the target translation language (which is English here).

2. The performance profile of CGC-QG (B6) deviates notably from that of all competing baselines: it attains the highest METEOR and ROUGE-L scores across all evaluated methods, yet ranks lowest on BLEU-1. Since CGC-QG relies on word-level content selection, we find it consistently introduces a large number of extraneous tokens into generated questions, resulting in overly long outputs (a mean length of 33.7 tokens, compared to 17.7 tokens for ground-truth questions and 19.3 tokens for our proposed model) that are either unanswerable or contain semantic inconsistencies. Our approach mitigates this error substantially via node-level content selection guided by semantic relational structure, as quantified in Table 3.
3. While both the SRL-based and DP-based semantic graph architectures (P1 and P2, respectively) achieve state-of-the-art BLEU performance, the DP-based graph model (P2) yields a small but consistent performance gain, with a 3.3% improvement in BLEU-4. A plausible explanation for this gap is that SRL parsing is unable to encode fine-grained semantic information within the graph structure, as SRL parsers frequently produce nodes that comprise long, unsegmented token sequences.
source_text: 2. The results of CGC-QG (B6) exhibits an unusual pattern compared with other methods, achieving the best METEOR and ROUGE-L but worst BLEU-1 among all baselines. As CGC-QG performs word-level content selection, we observe that it tends to include many irrelevant words in the question, leading to lengthy questions (33.7 tokens on average, while 17.7 for ground-truth questions and 19.3 for our model) that are unanswerable or with semantic errors. Our model greatly reduces the error with node-level content selection based on semantic relations (shown in Table 3 ). 3. While both SRL-based and DP-based semantic graph models (P1 and P2) achieve state-of-theart BLEU, DP-based graph (P2) performs slightly better (+3.3% in BLEU-4). A possible explanation is that SRL fails to include fine-grained semantic information into the graph, as the parsing often results in nodes containing a long sequence of tokens.

text:
### Data
We evaluate non-autoregressive machine translation (NAR-MT) training on both the WMT16 English-Romanian (En-Ro, ~610,000 sentence pairs) and WMT14 English-German (En-De, ~4.5 million sentence pairs) parallel corpora, alongside the corresponding official WMT baseline models for the En→Ro, Ro→En, En→De, and De→En translation directions, as well as the NAT-FT baseline introduced in Gu et al. (2017).<sup>27</sup> All reported results are computed on held-out test sets. For our En-Ro experiments, we use the Romanian segment of the 2015 News Crawl corpus and the English segment of the Europarl v7/v8 corpus as monolingual text sources<sup>2</sup>; both collections are roughly four times the size of the original En-Ro parallel training data. For En-De experiments, we draw German and English monolingual text from the 2007 and 2008 News Crawl corpora respectively<sup>2</sup>, and downsample these datasets to ~3 million sentences per language. Full dataset statistics are summarized in Table 1. Monolingual data is preprocessed following the pipeline outlined in Lee et al. (2018), consisting of tokenization and segmentation into subword units as described in Sennrich et al. (2015b). We employ a shared source-target vocabulary of approximately 40,000 units across all experiments, and evaluate translation quality using the BLEU metric.<sup>3</sup>
source_text: Data We evaluate NAR-MT training on both the WMT16 En-Ro (around 610k sentence pairs) and the WMT14 En-De (around 4.5M sentence pairs) parallel corpora along with the associated WMT Models WMT16 WMT14 En→Ro Ro→En En→De De→En NAT-FT (Gu et al., 2017) 27 We report all results on test sets. We used the Romanian portion of the News Crawl 2015 corpus and the English portion of the Europarl v7/v8 corpus 2 as monolingual text for our En-Ro experiments, which are both about 4 times larger than the original paired data. We used the News Crawl 2007/2008 corpora for German and English monolingual text 2 in our En-De experiments, and downsampled them to ∼3 million sentences per language. The data statistics are summarized in Table 1 . The monolingual data are processed following Lee et al. (2018) , which are tokenized and segmented into subword units (Sennrich et al., 2015b) . The vocabulary is shared between source and target languages and has ∼40k units. We use BLEU to evaluate the translation quality 3 .

text: GSL: Our model equipped with the GSL module achieves an F1 score of 48.91%, outperforming the baseline model. While GSL does not surpass the performance of ELMo, it delivers results that are fully comparable to those of ELMo. This observation indicates that the GSL and SFL modules are mutually reinforcing, as they utilize geometric structure information via algebraic operations. Furthermore, we leverage geometric structure cues to enhance discourse semantic understanding from a novel angle, a key difference from ELMo, which exclusively focuses on the inherent semantic information of the input text itself.
source_text: GSL: The F 1 score of our model using the GSL module is 48.91%, higher than the performance of Baseline. In addition, compared with ELMo, although the performance of GSL does not exceed ELMo's, GSL obtain comparable results. This manifests that the two modules (GSL and SFL) could reinforce with each other, which utilizes the geometric structure information by the algebraic operation. Moreover, we exploit the geometric structure clues to augment the semantic understanding of discourse from a new aspect, which is different from the ELMo only focusing on the semantic information of the text itself.

text: Text memories are computed via a Sentence Encoder at the onset of the full parsing pipeline, while graph memories are constructed incrementally by a Graph Encoder as parsing progresses. During iterative inference, a semantic representation of the current system state is applied to attend to both graph and text memories (marked by blue and red arrows) to locate new concepts and derive their relational connections to the existing graph, with these two information streams subsequently undergoing mutual refinement. Intuitively, following an initial pass over the input sentence and current graph state, targeted subregions of both the text sequence and graph structure are revisited to build a more robust understanding of the current parsing context. Subsequent inference steps typically perform fine-grained, targeted reads of the input text guided by explicit operational objectives, either confirming or overturning previously formulated hypotheses. Finally, after several iterations of these reasoning steps, the refined sequence and graph parsing decisions are leveraged to support graph expansion.
source_text: The text memories can be computed by Sentence Encoder at the beginning of the whole parsing while the graph memories are constructed by Graph Encoder incrementally as the parsing progresses. During the iterative inference, a semantic representation of current state is used to attend to both graph and text memories (blue and red arrows) in order to locate the new concept and obtain its relations to the existing graph, both of which subsequently refine each other. Intuitively, after a first glimpse of the input sentence and the current graph, specific sub-areas of both sequence and graph are revisited to obtain a better understanding of the current situation. Later steps typically read the text in detail with specific learning aims, either confirming or overturning a previous hypothesis. Finally, after several iterations of reasoning steps, the refined sequence/graph decisions are used for graph expansion.

text: To quantify the relevance of Common Crawl-derived supplementary articles to their corresponding assigned summaries, we randomly sampled 350 articles for manual annotation. For each sample, we compared the article’s title and first three sentences against its assigned summary, and categorized each article into one of three mutually exclusive classes: 1) on-topic, for articles centered on the exact event described in the corresponding summary; 2) related, for articles that reference the event but prioritize other content such as follow-up developments; and 3) unrelated, for articles with no reference to the event whatsoever. This annotation process yielded a distribution of 52% on-topic, 30% related, and 18% unrelated articles across the sampled set. We consider this level of noise acceptable, as it closely aligns with the noise profiles observed in real-world automatic content aggregation applications. Additionally, our experimental results confirm that these supplementary articles improve summarization performance (see Section 5 for full details).
source_text: To investigate how related the additional articles obtained from Common Crawl are to the summary they are assigned to, we randomly select 350 for manual annotation. We compare the article title and the first three sentences to the assigned summary, and pick one of the following three options: 1) "on-topic" if the article focuses on the event described in the summary, 2) "related" if the article mentions the event, but focuses on something else, e.g., follow-up, and 3) "unrelated" if there is no mention of the event. This results in 52% on-topic, 30% related, and 18% unrelated articles. We think that this amount of noise is acceptable, as it resembles noise present in applications with automatic content aggregation. Furthermore, summarization performance benefits from the additional articles in our experiments (see Section 5).

text: Similar to GPT-2, DIALOGPT is structured as an autoregressive (AR) language model, with a multi-layer transformer as its core architectural backbone. It diverges from GPT-2, however, in its training corpus, which consists of large-scale dialogue pairs and full conversational sessions extracted from public Reddit discussion threads. We hypothesize that this training data configuration allows DIALOGPT to model the joint probability distribution P(Target, Source) of turn-taking in conversational flows at a finer level of granularity. Empirical results align with this hypothesis: outputs generated by DIALOGPT are diverse, contain information specific to the input source prompt, and are analogous to the coherent outputs GPT-2 produces for long-form continuous text. We evaluated the pre-trained DIALOGPT model across two test sets: the publicly available DSTC-7 benchmark dataset, and a newly curated 6k multi-reference test set sourced from Reddit postings. DIALOGPT attains state-of-the-art results in both automatic and human evaluation, pushing overall performance to near-human conversational response quality levels.
source_text: Like GPT-2, DIALOGPT is formulated as an autoregressive (AR) language model, and uses the multi-layer transformer as model architecture. Unlike GPT-2, however, DIALOGPT is trained on large-scale dialogue pairs/sessions extracted from Reddit discussion chains. Our assumption is that this should enable DIALOGPT to capture the joint distribution of P (Target, Source) in conversational flow with finer granularity. In practice, this is what we observe: sentences generated by DIALOGPT are diverse and contain information specific to the source prompt, analogous what GPT-2 generates for continuous text. We have evaluated the pre-trained model on a public benchmark dataset (DSTC-7), and a new 6k multireference test dataset extracted from Reddit postings. DIALOGPT achieves state-of-the-art results in both automatic and human evaluation, lifting performance to near-human response quality.

text: Abstract Meaning Representation (AMR; Banarescu et al., 2013) is a broad-coverage semantic formalism that maps the meaning of individual sentences to rooted, directed, labeled graphs, in which nodes correspond to concepts and edges denote relational links between those concepts (see Figure 1 for an illustrative example). AMR parsing refers to the computational task of converting input natural language text into its corresponding AMR graph structure. One of the most pressing challenges in AMR parsing stems from the absence of explicit, predefined alignments between concept nodes in the output graph and surface words in the input text. This core property not only introduces substantial barriers to accurate concept prediction, but also creates a tight interdependency between the subtasks of concept prediction and relation prediction.
source_text: Abstract Meaning Representation (AMR) (Banarescu et al., 2013) is a broad-coverage semantic formalism that encodes the meaning of a sentence as a rooted, directed, and labeled graph, where nodes represent concepts and edges represent relations (See an example in Figure 1 ). AMR parsing is the task of transforming natural language text into AMR. One biggest challenge of AMR parsing is the lack of explicit alignments between nodes (concepts) in the graph and words in the text. This characteristic not only poses great difficulty in concept prediction but also brings a close tie for concept prediction and relation prediction.

text: Named Entity Recognition (NER) serves as a core component of many natural language processing (NLP) pipelines, and is deployed for a broad range of use cases including information extraction (Raiman and Raiman, 2018), question answering (Mollá et al., 2006), document de-identification (Stubbs et al., 2015), machine translation (Ugawa et al., 2018), and even conversational models (Ghazvininejad et al., 2018). For any given input document, the core objective of NER is to identify and classify text spans that reference entities belonging to pre-specified categories such as persons, organisations, and geographical locations.
source_text: Named Entity Recognition (NER) constitutes a core component in many NLP pipelines and is employed in a broad range of applications such as information extraction (Raiman and Raiman, 2018) , question answering (Mollá et al., 2006) , document de-identification (Stubbs et al., 2015) , machine translation (Ugawa et al., 2018) and even conversational models (Ghazvininejad et al., 2018) . Given a document, the goal of NER is to identify and classify spans referring to an entity belonging to pre-specified categories such as persons, organisations or geographical locations.

text: Natural language understanding (NLU) and natural language generation (NLG) constitute two core foundational tasks underpinning the development of task-oriented dialogue systems. Within contemporary dialogue system architectures, the NLU module first transforms a user utterance output by an automatic speech recognition model into a structured formal representation. This structured representation is subsequently processed by a downstream dialogue state tracker to revise the system’s belief state, a construct that encodes the cumulative inferred user goal. Using the current belief state as input, a policy network determines the formal representation of the intended system response. This formal response representation is ultimately fed to the NLG module, which produces the final natural language system response (Young et al., 2010).
source_text: Natural language understanding (NLU) and natural language generation (NLG) are two fundamental tasks in building task-oriented dialogue systems. In a modern dialogue system, an NLU module first converts a user utterance, provided by an automatic speech recognition model, into a formal representation. The representation is then consumed by a downstream dialogue state tracker to update a belief state which represents an aggregated user goal. Based on the current belief state, a policy network decides the formal representation of the system response. This is finally used by an NLG module to generate the system response (Young et al., 2010) .

text: Initial annotations extracted by the RBS were manually corrected and expanded by a biology domain expert using the BRAT annotation interface (Stenetorp et al., 2012). We formally define three distinct relation types for this task: TRADE-OFF, ARGUMENT-MODIFIER, and NOT-A-TRADE-OFF. The last of these categories describes phrases associated with a trigger word, but not connected via a TRADE-OFF relation. These syntactically similar relation instances serve as useful negative samples that provide valuable training signal. Negative samples are critically important because candidate trigger words can appear in contiguous multi-word sequences: for example, the phrase “negative correlation” denotes a TRADE-OFF relation, whereas the standalone term “correlation” does not. This property increases the difficulty of training example annotation, and lexical and syntactic patterns that reliably signify the target relation are relatively sparse (Peng et al., 2017). For the sake of simplicity, with minor abuse of terminology, we refer to all three of these relation types collectively as trade-offs.
source_text: The initial annotations extracted by the RBS were manually corrected and extended by a biology expert using the BRAT interface (Stenetorp et al., 2012) . We define three relation types: TRADE-OFF, ARGUMENT-MODIFIER and NOT-A-TRADE-OFF. The latter denotes phrases that are related to a trigger word, but not by a TRADE-OFF relation. These syntactically similar relations provide useful training signal as negative samples. Negative samples are important because possible trigger words can be contiguous, e.g., the phrase 'negative correlation' denotes a TRADE-OFF relation, whereas 'correlation' by itself does not. As a result, the annotation of training examples is harder, and lexical and syntactic patterns that correctly signify the relation are sparse (Peng et al., 2017) . For simplicity's sake, with some abuse of terminology, we refer to all such relations collectively as trade-offs.

text: On the MuTual benchmark, both BERT and RoBERTa outperform all competing baseline models, a finding that aligns with results reported in prior related work (Talmor et al., 2019). This performance edge arises primarily from the reasoning capabilities these models acquire during pre-training on large-scale text corpora. Though RoBERTa only achieves an R@1 score of 71.3%, it posts a striking R@2 result of 89.2%, which indicates the model is capable of placing the correct response within the top two ranked candidate positions. BERT-MC and RoBERTa-MC yield performance outcomes nearly identical to those of their base BERT and RoBERTa counterparts, respectively. Even the strongest performing model, RoBERTa, still trails human performance by 23 points on the R@1 metric, however, demonstrating that MuTual is a highly challenging dataset that opens new avenues for addressing underexplored, complex reasoning problems in multi-turn conversational settings.
source_text: Both BERT and RoBERTa outperform other models in MuTual, which is consistent with results in other literatures (Talmor et al., 2019) . This is mainly because models learn reasoning capability during the pre-training on a large corpus. Although RoBERTa only gets 71.3% on R@1, it achieves a surprising number, 89.2 %, on R@2, indicating that the model is able to rank the correct response to the top-2 position. BERT-MC and RoBERTa-MC obtain similar results with BERT and RoBERTa, respectively. However, even RoBERTa is far behind human performance 23 points on R@1, indicating that MuTual is indeed a challenging dataset, which opens the door for tackling new and complex reasoning problems in multi-turn conversations.

text: Our final evaluation task centers on characterizing the grammatical functions of relation arguments. The two grammatical role subtasks, GRHead and GRTail, require assigning the correct role to each argument, operationalized as the dependency label linking the argument token to its corresponding syntactic head. This task is motivated by the well-documented observation that the subject and object positions of verbal predicates frequently align with relation arguments for a wide range of relation types, as illustrated by the example “Bayer acquired Monsanto”. In our current experimental setup, we evaluate performance across four target dependency roles: nsubj, nsubjpass, dobj, and iobj, with all remaining dependency labels aggregated into a single “other” category. We note that additional grammatical relation types are also of significant interest for relation extraction research, including possessive modifiers (exemplified by “Google's Larry Page”), noun-noun compounds (as in “Google CEO Larry Page”), and appositive constructions (seen in “Larry Page, CEO of Google”).
source_text: Our last task concerns the grammatical function of relation arguments. The grammatical role tasks (GRHead, GRTail) ask for the role of each argument, as given by the dependency label connecting the argument and its syntactic head token. The motivation is that the subject and object of verbal constructions often correspond to relation arguments for some relation types, e.g. "Bayer acquired Monsanto". We currently test for four roles, namely nsubj, nsubjpass, dobj and iobj, and group all other dependency labels into the other class. Note that there are other grammatical relations that may be of interest for relation extraction, for example possessive modifiers ("Google's Larry Page"), compounds ("Google CEO Larry Page"), and appositions ("Larry Page, CEO of Google").

text: We provide our annotators with explicit instructions to consistently associate slot fillers with the syntactically closest EXPERIMENT mention. When an experiment’s description spans more than one clause, we connect the two corresponding EXPERIMENT mentions using the same exp relational label. We employ the exp variation relation to link experiments performed on the same cell, but under slightly divergent operating conditions. This exp variation link type may also relate two frame-evoking elements that reference two distinct measurements conducted on different materials or cells, but under identical experimental conditions. In such instances, these frame-evoking elements typically encode a comparative meaning, as illustrated by terms such as “increase” or “reach from … to.”
source_text: We instruct our annotators to always link slot fillers to the syntactically closest EXPERIMENT mention. If the description of an experiment spans more than one clause, we link the two relevant EXPERIMENTs using the relation same exp. We use exp variation to link experiments done on the same cell, but with slightly different operating conditions. The link type exp variation can also relate two frameevoking elements that refer to two measurements performed on different materials/cells, but in the same experimental conditions. In this case, the frame-evoking elements usually convey an idea of comparison, e.g., "increase" or "reach from ... to."

text: However, the source article reports only the approximate value “nearly 340,000 yuan”, while the CLSMT-generated summary incorrectly renders this as a precise figure, an inappropriate deviation from the source content. Both the CLSMT and CLSMS generated summaries also exhibit the drawback of containing redundant information, as their output is overly lengthy. The summary generated by our proposed ATS-A method matches the reference summary most closely, and captures nearly all key informational points from the source article. These findings confirm that our approach can generate summaries with more accurate semantic content than the evaluated baseline models.
source_text: ever, the amount in the source article is an approximate value "nearly 340,000 yuan", while CLSMTgenerated summary directly expresses the exact value, which is inappropriate. The downside to both CLSMT-generated summary and CLSMSgenerated summary is that they contain redundant information, since they are relatively lengthy. The summary generated by our ATS-A method matches the reference best and nearly captures all the key points in the source article. In conclusion, our method can generate summaries with more accurate semantics than baselines.

text: Initialization with Transformer-based Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) Encoder. The Libri-Trans corpus comprises 236 hours of English read speech paired with corresponding verbatim transcriptions and French translations. For model training, we utilized the corpus’s 100-hour clean speech subset, and augmented training set translation references using Google Translate outputs, consistent with protocols established in prior work (Bérard et al., 2018; Bahar et al., 2019a, 2019b). We report case-insensitive BLEU scores computed on the held-out test set for all evaluations. A shared 1,000-token vocabulary was employed across all experimental tasks to ensure consistent comparison. Full experimental results are presented in Table 3. Notably, all evaluated models were trained on identical data resources, and their performance is competitive with published findings from prior work.
source_text: Transformer + ASR encoder init. Libri-trans corpus contains 236-hours of English read speech, the corresponding transcription, and the French translations . We used the clean 100-hours of speech data and augmented translation references with Google Translate for the training set (Bérard et al., 2018; Bahar et al., 2019a,b) . We report case-insensitive BLEU on the test set. We used 1k vocabulary for all tasks. Results are shown in Table 3 . Note that all models used the same data resource and are competitive to previous work.

text: Filtering: For each word \(w_i\) in the input text, we retrieve from our precompiled wordlists (see §4) a set of candidate diacritizations \(D_i = \{d_{i1}, ..., d_{ik}\}\) and their corresponding morphological analyses. This candidate set is then further refined by intersecting it with the predicted morphological analysis generated for the target word. Words absent from our wordlists are assigned an empty set, indicating that no constraints are placed on their diacritization. Following this stage, we retain an average of 1.2 valid diacritic sequences for each word present in the wordlists. If we were to select uniformly at random from these filtered candidate sets, we would obtain an 87.1% word-level exact-match diacritization accuracy on our Modern Hebrew test corpus.
source_text: Filtering For each word w i in the text, we retrieve from our wordlists (see §4) a set of possible diacritizations D i = d i 1 , ..., d i and their corresponding morphological analyses. This set is then further refined by intersecting it with the predicted morphological analysis for the word. Words that are not in our list get an empty set, indicating that their diacritization is not constrained. This stage leaves us with an average of 1.2 diacritic sequences for each known word. If we were to perform random selection from this list, we would achieve 87.1% exact-match word-level diacritization accuracy on our Modern Hebrew test corpus.

text: Our results demonstrate that the proposed model underperforms on tasks that require external domain knowledge and access to broader contextual information than is supplied by the input method alone. For example, generating an accurate updated comment for the method depicted in Figure 3 depends on implicit knowledge that the get() function returns the element at the first index when no positional argument is specified. As this information is not available to our model, it produces an implausible comment revision: “@return complex in given position.” By contrast, the reranked generation model outputs “@return the complex value”, a revision that is defensibly coherent given the available contextual cues. This finding indicates that expanding the scope of code context supplied as input would improve performance for both model architectures.
