Additional analyses reveal that the proposed model also exhibits higher error rates when tasked with reasoning over substantial code modifications between the legacy version (M_old) and updated version (M_new) of a method; across most of these test cases, the reranked generation model produced higher-quality outputs. This pattern implies that when code edits are sufficiently extensive, M_new functionally operates as an entirely new method, making de novo comment generation a more suitable strategy than targeted revision. An ensembled framework that combines our revision system with standalone generation, mediated by a regression model that predicts the required magnitude of comment edit, would likely yield a more generalizable solution capable of handling such edge cases. Sample outputs for these test cases are presented in Appendix C.
source_text: We find that our model performs poorly in cases requiring external knowledge and more context than that provided by the given method. For instance, correctly updating the comment shown in Figure 3 requires knowing that get returns the item in the first position if no argument is provided. Our model does not have access to this information, and it fails to generate a reasonable update: "@return complex in given position." On the other hand, the reranked generation model produces "@return the complex value" which is arguably reasonable for the given context. This suggests that incorporating more code context could be beneficial for both models. Furthermore, we find that our model tends to make more mistakes when it must reason about a large amount of code change between M old and M new , and we found that in many such cases, the output of the reranked generation model was better. This suggests that when there are substantial code changes, M new effectively becomes a new method, and generating a comment from scratch may be more appropriate. Ensembling generation with our system through a regression model that predicts the extent of editing that is needed may lead to a more generalizable approach that can accommodate such cases. Sample outputs are given in Appendix C.

text: For adversarial training (AT), the pseudo-sentence generation step and neural machine translation (NMT) training step are executed in an interleaved manner. As a concrete illustration, consider the target-language perspective AT pipeline depicted in Figure 1(a): we first sample a batch of anchored source sentences, decode these inputs to generate corresponding pseudo target sentences, and then leverage the identical batch to optimize the target-to-anchored-source NMT model. Concurrently, a separate batch of target sentences is decoded to produce pseudo anchored source sentences, after which we use this same batch to train the anchored-source-to-target NMT model. This paired two-way update process is repeated iteratively until the AT workflow reaches convergence.
source_text: For AT, the pseudo sentence generation step and NMT training step are interleaved. Take the target language view AT shown in Figure 1 (a) for example, we extract anchored source sentences as one batch, and decode them into pseudo target sentences; then we use the same batch to train the NMT model of target-to-anchored source. In the meantime, a batch of target sentences are decoded into pseudo anchored source sentences, and then we use the same batch to train the NMT model of anchored source-to-target. The above process repeats until AT converges.

text: The model yields improved language accuracy, with zero-shot accuracy gains of +7.78% and +13.81% for the 3→5 and 3→4 experimental conditions respectively in the absence of ROBT, as well as stronger zero-shot translation performance both prior to ROBT application (with zero-shot BLEU improvements of 1.22 and 0.53 for 3→5 and 3→4, no ROBT) and following ROBT integration (with corresponding zero-shot BLEU gains of 2.20 and 1.56 for 3→5 and 3→4, with ROBT). In short, increasing modeling capacity confers dual advantages for zero-shot translation, elevating both core performance and robustness. Turning to the convergence behavior of ROBT: unlike prior research (Gu et al., 2019; Lakew et al., 2019), we employ an online backtranslation framework in this work. The performance curve presented in Figure 1 confirms that ROBT is highly effective, requiring only several thousand training steps to converge, which suggests that decoding the full training corpus for each zero-shot language pair is unnecessary for effective implementation. We leave to future work the exploration of whether alternative backtranslation strategies (beyond greedy decoding) can deliver larger, sustained performance improvements when used in combination with ROBT.
source_text: language accuracy (+7.78%/+13.81% ACC zero , 3 → 5 / 3 → 4 , w/o ROBT) and deliver better zero-shot performance before (+1.22/+0.53 BLEU zero , 3 → 5 / 3 → 4 , w/o ROBT) and after ROBT (+2.20/+1.56 BLEU zero , 3 → 5 / 3 → 4 , w/ ROBT). In other words, increasing the modeling capacity benefits zero-shot translation and improves robustness. Convergence of ROBT. Unlike prior studies (Gu et al., 2019; Lakew et al., 2019) , we resort to an online method for backtranslation. The curve in Figure 1 shows that ROBT is very effective, and takes only a few thousand steps to converge, suggesting that it is unnecessary to decode the whole training set for each zero-shot language pair. We leave it to future work to explore whether different back-translation strategies (other than greedy decoding) will deliver larger and continued benefits with ROBT.

text: Most existing research on neural machine translation (NMT) leverages corpora composed of aligned parallel sentence pairs, operating under the assumption that models can implicitly acquire prior linguistic knowledge through attention mechanisms (Luong et al., 2015). However, work by Shi et al. (2006) demonstrates that such models remain unable to capture fine-grained deep structural linguistic features, and multiple independent studies (Sennrich and Haddow, 2016; Eriguchi et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2017, 2018) have established that integrating syntactic information can yield performance improvements for NMT systems. That said, the vast majority of existing syntax-informed NMT architectures are built on recurrent neural network (RNN) frameworks (Elman, 1990), and only a small number of recent works have explored syntax integration strategies specifically for the Transformer model (Vaswani et al., 2017). Wu et al. (2018) tested a method for integrating syntactic data into Transformer-based NMT, a design that required three separate encoders and two decoders in addition to target-language dependency relations, a constraint that renders the approach infeasible for low-resource target language settings. For their part, Zhang et al. (2019) incorporate source-language syntactic information by concatenating intermediate outputs generated by a dependency parser directly to input word embeddings. * Work completed during affiliation with Tokyo Institute of Technology.
source_text: Research in neural machine translation (NMT) has mostly exploited corpora consisting of pairs of parallel sentences, with the assumption that a model can automatically learn prior linguistic knowledge via an attention mechanism (Luong et al., 2015) . However, Shi et al. (2006) found that these models still fail to capture deep structural details, and several studies (Sennrich and Haddow, 2016; Eriguchi et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2017 Chen et al., , 2018 have shown that syntactic information has the potential to improve these models. Nevertheless, the majority of syntax-aware NMT models are based on recurrent neural networks (RNNs; Elman 1990), with only a few recent studies that have investigated methods for the Transformer model (Vaswani et al., 2017) . Wu et al. (2018) evaluated an approach to incorporate syntax in NMT with a Transformer model, which not only required three encoders and two decoders, but also target-side dependency relations (precluding its use to low-resource target languages). Zhang et al. (2019) integrate source-side syntax by concatenating the intermediate representations of a dependency parser to word embeddings. * Work done while at Tokyo Institute of Technology.

text: As illustrated in Figure 2, our proposed framework consists of two core components: an intuitive web-based user interface (UI) built to capture both annotation labels and natural language explanations for annotators’ labeling decisions, and a weak supervision pipeline that parses these explanations to generate weakly labeled training data. This weakly labeled dataset is combined with manually provided gold labels to train task-specific models that produce higher-quality annotation recommendations for subsequent unlabeled instances. The frontend UI displays unlabeled instances to annotators—these instances may be sampled using active learning strategies if specified—alongside precomputed annotation recommendations, a design intended to reduce overall annotation overhead. All model components are implemented in PyTorch, and a dedicated application programming interface (API) enables seamless communication between the web UI and the backend weak supervision framework. The framework’s learned parameters are updated incrementally via an online learning regime, yielding near-real-time performance improvements as new annotation data is collected. We first outline the design and functionality of the annotation UI in §3, before elaborating on the architecture and operational mechanics of our weak supervision framework in §4.
source_text: As shown in Fig. 2 , our framework consists of two main components, a user-friendly web-UI that can capture labels and explanations for labeling decisions, and a weak supervision framework that parses explanations for the creation of weakly labeled data. The framework then uses this weakly labeled data in conjunction with user-provided labels to train models for improved annotation recommendations. Our UI shows annotators unlabeled instances (can be sampled using active learning), along with annotation recommendations in an effort to reduce annotation costs. We use Py-Torch to build our models and implement an API for communication between the web-UI and our weak supervision framework. The learned parameters of our framework are updated in an online fashion, thus improving in near real time. We will first touch on the annotation UI ( §3) and then go into our weak supervision framework ( §4).

text: Locating core themes and key concepts within scientific publications is a resource-intensive activity for subject-matter experts, and an inaccessible challenge for non-specialist audiences (Alper et al., 2004; El-Arini and Guestrin, 2011; Pain, 2016). This difficulty is particularly acute in cross-disciplinary research areas, where practitioners working in a target field frequently lack in-depth familiarity with the foundational knowledge of the source domain being integrated (Carr et al., 2018). One concrete illustration of this dynamic occurs in biomimetics, an engineering-based problem-solving framework that leverages preexisting analogous solutions developed in biological systems (Kruiper et al., 2016). A core limitation in this use case is that engineers (the target domain) have minimal working knowledge of biology (the source domain) or specific structural and functional traits of plant species, as demonstrated in Figure 1: Example of Semi-Open Relation Extraction in the Biology domain (Burgess et al., 2006). For the illustrated use case, we first identify the TRADE-OFF relation articulated between the core concepts of ‘safety’ and ‘efficiency’ (marked in blue), which occurs exclusively in the context of ‘conifer species’ (marked in green). We then conduct deeper exploration of the target paper’s content by analyzing outputs generated by an Open Information Extraction (OIE) system, which are highlighted in red in the figure. The core concepts linked by the TRADE-OFF relation support the removal of a large volume of extraneous OIE outputs, which have been widely documented to have high error rates when applied to scientific textual corpora. Additional OIE extractions recovered from the same document can clarify the semantic definition of domain-relevant concepts, such as ‘xylem’, as shown in the red box positioned at the top of the figure.
source_text: Identifying the central theme and concepts in scientific texts is a time-consuming task for experts and a hard task for laymen (Alper et al., 2004; El-Arini and Guestrin, 2011; Pain, 2016) . This problem is even more pronounced in inter-disciplinary fields of study, where experts in a target domain often lack the deeper knowledge of a source domain (Carr et al., 2018) . A specific example is biomimetics, an engineering problem-solving process in which one draws on analogous biological solutions (Kruiper et al., 2016) . A major issue is that engineers (target domain) know little biology (source domain) or characteristics of plants or Figure 1 : Example of Semi-Open Relation Extraction in the Biology domain (Burgess et al., 2006) . We first extract the TRADE-OFF expressed between the central concepts 'safety' and 'efficiency' (in blue), that takes place specifically in 'conifer species' (in green). We further explore the content of a paper by investigating the results of an Open Information Extraction (OIE) system (in red). The central concepts captured by a TRADE-OFF mechanism enable the filtering of many irrelevant OIE extractions, which are found to be errorprone in scientific texts. Further OIE extractions found in the document can shed light on the semantic meaning of relevant concepts, e.g., ' xylem', as depicted at the top of the Figure ( in the red box).

text: A broad range of lexical items function as explicit indicators of trade-off relations, including compromise, optimization, balance, interplay, and conflict (Kruiper et al., 2018). We leverage these terms as trigger cues for a custom Rule-Based System (RBS), which we deploy across a corpus of 10,000 open-access publications sourced from the Journal of Experimental Biology (JEB) and BioMed Central (BMC) titles spanning the fields of Biology, Evolutionary Biology, and Systems Biology. These publication venues were selected exclusively to ensure all corpus entries are anchored in the biological research domain. For each collected paper, we extracted and retained only the abstract, introduction, results, discussion, and conclusion sections for downstream processing. We utilized the spaCy 3 NLP library to segment the extracted text into individual sentences, as well as to generate part-of-speech (POS) tags and dependency parse structures for each segmented unit. The final FOBIE dataset is comprised solely of sentences that our RBS classified as containing an explicit TRADE-OFF semantic relation.
source_text: A variety of words are able to indicate a trade-off, e.g., compromise, optimization, balance, interplay and conflict (Kruiper et al., 2018) . We adapt these terms as trigger words in a Rule-Based System (RBS) and run it on 10k open-access papers that were collected from the Journal of Experimental Biology (JEB) and BioMed Central (BMC) journals on 'Biology', 'Evolutionary Biology, and 'Systems Biology'. The selection of journals was made only to the extent that the articles focus on the biological domain. We retained the abstract, introduction, results, discussion and conclusion sections. We used spaCy 3 to split the texts into sentences and identify POS tags and dependency structure. The FOBIE dataset contains only sentences that the RBS identified as expressing a TRADE-OFF relation.

text: A beam size of 90 is selected via a grid search conducted on the German-English (De-En) language pair, and this value is held constant across all subsequent experiments. For the filtering step implemented during pre-expansion pruning, per-word conditional cross-entropy thresholds are fine-tuned independently for each language pair, as different languages inherently exhibit distinct entropy and cross-entropy properties. For the Bicleaner tool, we retain its full default parameter configurations, with the sole modification of deactivating its built-in language model filter. All models discussed in this work generate translations into English, though the proposed method is fundamentally language-agnostic. To enable comparisons of our method across reverse translation directions, we therefore train a separate English-to-German (En→De) model. Performance metrics for all our systems evaluated on the sample dataset are reported in Table 1. The proposed method achieves substantially higher precision scores relative to its recall scores. We hypothesize that if systems trained for opposite translation directions retrieve non-identical sets of sentence pairs, computing the union of their outputs will trade a portion of precision for improved recall, leading to an overall higher F1 score. Accordingly, we include the performance of the union of outputs from the En→De and De→En systems, marked with the label “(3) ∪ (4)”, in the same table. Similarly, we also compute the union of results obtained from cross-entropy filtering and Bicleaner filtering, and report its corresponding performance scores in Table 1 as well.
source_text: We choose beam size 90 by performing a grid search on De-En pair and keep it unchanged. Regarding the filtering for pre-expansion pruning, per-word conditional cross-entropy thresholds are tuned separately for each pair, because languages inherently have different (cross-)entropies. For Bicleaner, we stick to its default settings, except that we disable the language model filter. All our models translate into English, but our method is actually language-agnostic. Hence, we train a separate En→De model, which will allow us to compare our method in inverse translation directions. Table 1 reports the performance of our systems on the sample data. Our method exhibits a much higher precision than recall. We hypothesise that if the systems in inverse directions retrieve different sentence pairs, then taking a union will sacrifice some precision for recall, consequently a higher F1. Thus, we present in the same table the results of taking the union of outputs from En→De and De→En systems, labelled as "(3) ∪ (4)". Likewise, we also take the union of the results from cross-entropy and Bicleaner filtering and report scores in the same table.

text: Beyond our benchmarking of bilingual PLuGS model variants, we additionally train and assess a unified multilingual PLuGS instantiation that supports all five target non-English languages under a single framework, with the ability to produce generations in each of the five covered languages. Empirical results demonstrate that typologically similar languages mutually reinforce their representations in the shared embedding space, with measurable quantitative performance improvements observed for the Romance language cohort included in our experimental setup. A related yet comparatively unanticipated finding concerns the quality of English captions produced by PLuGS models (which we refer to as Stabilizer outputs): pairwise side-by-side human evaluations confirm these generations outperform captions output by the baseline monolingual English model.
source_text: Besides the evaluations on bilingual PLuGS models, we also train and evaluate a multilingual PLuGS model, in which all five non-English languages considered are supported through a single model capable of generating outputs in all 5 languages. The results indicate that similar languages are reinforcing each other in the common representation space, showing quantitative gains for the Romance languages involved in our experiments. A related but perhaps less expected result is that the English captions generated by PLuGS models (what we call the Stablizer outputs) are better, as measured using side-by-side human evaluations, than captions generated by the original, monolingual English model.

text: Label smoothing has yielded state-of-the-art models for image classification (Szegedy et al., 2016), language translation (Vaswani et al., 2017), and speech recognition (Chorowski and Jaitly, 2017). While existing applications of label smoothing (Szegedy et al., 2016; Vaswani et al., 2017; Chorowski and Jaitly, 2017; Müller et al., 2019) center on categorical labels to prevent networks from developing excessive predictive confidence, the present work implements label smoothing for ordinal labels (i.e., Likert scores) to prevent networks from overfitting to noisy ordinal label data.
source_text: Label smoothing has led to state-of-the-art models in image classification (Szegedy et al., 2016) , language translation (Vaswani et al., 2017) and speech recognition (Chorowski and Jaitly, 2017) . Prior attempts in label smoothing (Szegedy et al., 2016; Vaswani et al., 2017; Chorowski and Jaitly, 2017; Müller et al., 2019) focus on categorical labels to prevent the network from becoming overconfident while we apply label smoothing on ordinal labels (i.e., Likert scores) to prevent the network from overfitting on noisy ordinal labels.

text: For the BASHI dataset, Hou (2018a) documented a strict accuracy score of 29.94% derived from mentions automatically extracted from gold-standard syntactic tree annotations. Our proposed BARQA system, when run without incorporating any mention or semantic information, yields a strict accuracy of 32.27% under the identical evaluation protocol. BARQA’s performance improves further to a strict accuracy of 38.66% when mention and semantic features are integrated into the model architecture. It is worth noting that Hou (2018a) also adapted their deterministic algorithm for lexical bridging anaphora resolution on the ARRAU corpus (Poesio et al., 2018), reporting an accuracy of 32.39% when evaluated on the RST Test dataset. While the present work does not center on lexical bridging anaphora as a core task, our BARQA model is also generalizable to this resolution task. Experimental results show that BARQA, when trained exclusively on the RST Train dataset containing approximately 2,000 question-answer pairs, obtains an accuracy of 34.59% on the held-out RST Test dataset.
source_text: On BASHI, Hou (2018a) reported an accuracy of 29.94% (strict accuracy) using automatically extracted mentions from the gold syntactic tree annotations. Our system BARQA without any mention/semantic information achieves an accuracy of 32.27% using the same strict accuracy evaluation. The result of BARQA is further improved with an accuracy of 38.66% when we integrate mention/semantic information into the model. Note that Hou (2018a) also adapted their deterministic algorithm to resolve lexical bridging anaphors on ARRAU (Poesio et al., 2018) and reported an accuracy of 32.39% on the RST Test dataset. Although in this paper we do not focus on lexical bridging, our model BARQA can also be applied to resolve lexical bridging anaphors. We found that BARQA trained on the RST Train dataset alone with around 2,000 QA pairs achieves an accuracy of 34.59% on the RST Test dataset.

text: In this work, we introduce a Dilated Convolutional Attention network with N-gram Matching Mechanism (DCANM) designed for the automatic ICD coding task. The full architectural blueprint of DCANM is visualized in Figure 3. The model takes as input the full concatenated sequence of all sentences extracted from input clinical notes. First, the input token sequence undergoes explicit semantic feature extraction via cross-interaction with the canonical names of target ICD codes, a step that produces an n-gram matching matrix as an intermediate output. In parallel, the input clinical text sequence is projected into dense embedding vectors and fed through a dilated convolutional neural network (CNN) module to extract implicit contextual semantic features. A learnable attention module is integrated into the pipeline to further boost predictive performance by prioritizing clinically relevant text segments. All extracted explicit and implicit feature representations are subsequently concatenated to construct the final combined feature vector for each input sample. As a final predictive step, a sigmoid-activated classifier layer maps the combined feature vector to a per-code probability score for all candidate ICD codes. We provide granular implementation and architectural details for each module in the following subsections.
source_text: We propose a Dilated Convolutional Attention network with N-gram Matching Mechanism (DCANM) for ICD coding task. Figure 3 describes the architecture of the model. The input of model is all sentences in clinical notes, which are spliced together. The input sentences interact with ICD code names to capture explicit semantic features and generate an n-gram matrix. At the same time, the input sentences are transformed into vector and processed by dilated CNN to capture implicit semantic features. Attention mechanism is used to improve the performance. Then all features are concatenated to form the final features. Finally, we use a sigmoid classifier to predict the probability of each code. Next, we give the detailed descriptions.

text: For every dialogue in the training set, we introduce controlled perturbations to the dialogue flow to generate synthetic invalid dialogues, then train the model to distinguish between real and perturbed instances. Dialogue flow is a user-centric metric that captures whether a conversation progresses naturally and coherently, often described colloquially as “going smoothly”. To perturb the flow of a given target dialogue \(x_i\), we randomly select one user utterance in \(x_i\) and substitute it with an arbitrary user utterance sampled from the training corpus \(D_{\text{train}}\), producing the perturbed dialogue \(x_{i,\text{fake}}\). With high probability, this substitution renders the system utterance immediately following the replaced user turn semantically or pragmatically inappropriate. Accordingly, we incorporate instances of the form \(\{(x_i, x_{i,\text{fake}}, z = 1)\}\) into our pool of training pairs. We follow an identical protocol to generate a second perturbed dialogue for each \(x_i\), this time replacing a randomly selected system utterance rather than a user turn. This procedure yields two perturbed dialogues per original training instance, resulting in a total of \(2N_{\text{train}}\) real-fake dialogue pairs overall; a sample perturbation is provided in Table 3. We note that response appropriateness is among the most widely adopted metrics for human evaluation of conversational systems (Lowe et al., 2017). By training CMADE to distinguish between perturbed and unmodified original dialogues, we aim to learn a robust dialogue encoder \(\phi\) that maps dialogues with comparable flow characteristics to nearby representations in the shared feature space.
source_text: For each dialog in the training set, we perturb the dialog flow to generate a fake dialog and train the model to differentiate the fake dialog and the real one. Dialog flow is a usercentric measure of whether a conversation is "going smoothly" . To perturb the dialog flow for each dialog x i , we randomly replace a user utterance in x i with a random user utterance from the training corpus D train , yielding a perturbed dialog x i,f ake . With high probability, the system utterance immediately following the replaced user utterance becomes inappropriate. Therefore, we incorporate {(x i , x i,f ake , z = 1)} into the training pairs. Similarly, we also randomly replace a system utterance and yield another perturbed dialog. We generate two perturbed dialogs for each dialog in the training set and thus 2N train real-fake dialog pairs in total. An example is shown in Table 3 . We note that appropriateness is one of the most widely applied metrics of human evaluation on dialogs (Lowe et al., 2017) . By learning to differentiate the perturbed dialog and the original one, we expect CMADE to learn a good dialog encoder φ which maps dialogs with similar dialog flow close to each other in the feature space.

text: We developed a drug-drug interaction (DDI) detection model designed to classify whether a given candidate input sentence provides evidence of a functional interaction between two identified drug entities. This DDI detection framework leverages pre-trained Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT; Devlin et al., 2018) architectures to encode input text sequences. BERT-derived models are well-documented to exhibit strong cross-domain transfer performance, and can attain high task accuracy even when fine-tuned on limited volumes of task-specific annotated training data. For this implementation, we specifically use the large variant of the pre-trained RoBERTa model, an optimized iteration of the core BERT architecture with approximately 340 million trainable parameters (Liu et al., 2019). We fine-tune the pre-trained contextual embeddings of this base RoBERTa language model using labeled DDI classification training data, and designate the resulting fine-tuned system as RoBERTa-DDI.
source_text: We train a DDI-detection model to predict whether a given candidate sentence provides evidence of an interaction between two drug entities. Our DDI-detection model uses pre-trained BERT models (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) (Devlin et al., 2018) to encode input sequences. These models have been shown to be effective at domain transfer, and are able to achieve high performance using small amounts of task-specific annotated data. In particular, we use the large version of the pre-trained RoBERTa model, a further-optimized BERT model, that has approximately 340M parameters (Liu et al., 2019) . We fine-tune the pre-trained embeddings of the RoBERTa language model using labeled data for DDI classification, and we call the resulting model RoBERTa-DDI.

text: As BPE-dropout generates more fine-grained segmentation outputs, sentences processed with BPE-dropout have higher total token counts; the full distribution of these sentence lengths is presented in Figure 4(a), where for a dropout rate p = 0.1, segmented sentences are approximately 1.25 times longer on average. This introduces a hypothetical risk that models trained on BPE-dropout segmented data may default to finer-grained segmentation during inference, consequently reducing inference speed. Empirical results do not support this concern, however: the length distributions of translations generated by models trained on standard BPE and BPE-dropout segmented data are nearly identical, as illustrated in Figure 4(b). Footnote 8 and Table 4 validate these observations, demonstrating that inference latency for models trained with BPE-dropout does not differ substantially from that of models trained with standard BPE. Inference time for models with a larger vocabulary is more than 1.4 times longer than that of models with a 32k vocabulary.
source_text: Since BPE-dropout produces more fine-grained segmentation, sentences segmented with BPEdropout are longer; distribution of sentence lengths are shown in Figure 4 (a) (with p = 0.1, on average about 1.25 times longer). Thus there is a potential danger that models trained with BPEdropout may tend to use more fine-grained segmentation in inference and hence to slow inference down. However, in practice this is not the case: distributions of lengths of generated translations for models trained with BPE and with BPEdropout are close (Figure 4 (b) ). 8 Table 4 confirms these observations and shows that inference time of models trained with BPEdropout is not substantially different from the ones trained with BPE. ulary is more than 1.4 times longer than models with 32k vocabulary.

text: Our proposed Dialogue Management (DM) system is built on a directed graph structure that formalizes both dialog state definitions and state evolution dynamics: the finite enumerated set of all possible dialog states forms the graph’s node set, while all pre-specified permissible state transitions correspond to the graph’s edge set. We instantiate this graph with a single unique global start node and support multiple distinct end nodes to accommodate varied task termination conditions. Each node’s outgoing edges are either directly associated with the semantic enumeration index described in preceding sections, or explicitly marked as a RepeatedFail edge. The DM module traverses edges of the first type when the associated semantic label is returned by the upstream semantic parser, and triggers traversal of a RepeatedFail edge when the semantic parser has returned REJECT outputs for n consecutive turns, where n is a configurable hyperparameter set by the system designer.
source_text: Our Dialogue Management (DM) system is based on a directed graph, representing the definition and evolution of dialog state by enumerating the finite set of all possible states (the graph nodes), and the allowed transitions between them (the graph edges). We assume a unique global start node and allow multiple end nodes. A node's outgoing edges are either directly associated with a semantic enum index as described above or marked as a RepeatedFail edge. The DM traverses the former when its outbound edge is returned by the semantic parser and the latter when the semantic parser has returned REJECT n times in a row, for some author controlled n.

text: To investigate the validity of Likert-score-based dialog evaluation, we first conduct a large-scale analysis of 3,608 curated real-world human-machine dialogs and their associated self-reported Likert scale ratings collected as part of the Amazon Alexa Prize Challenge (Ram et al., 2018a; Chen et al., 2018). A notable property of these ratings is their J-shaped skewed distribution: nearly half of all dialogs are assigned the maximum possible Likert score. The prevalence of such extreme rating distributions has long been documented by the business research community across a wide range of real-world contexts (Schoenmüller et al., 2018; Godes and Silva, 2012; Hu et al., 2017; Zervas et al., 2015).
source_text: To explore the validity of Likert score based dialog evaluation, we first perform a large-scale data analysis of 3,608 collected real-world humanmachine dialogs along with their self-reported Likert scale ratings from Amazon Alexa Prize Challenge (Ram et al., 2018a; Chen et al., 2018) . One noticeable property of the ratings is its J-shape skew distribution: nearly half of the dialogs are rated with the highest Likert score. The prevalence of such extreme distribution of ratings has long been observed by the business research community in variable aspects of reallife (Schoenmüller et al., 2018; Godes and Silva, 2012; Hu et al., 2017; Zervas et al., 2015) .

text: In this work, we introduce the Wikipedia Current Events Portal (WCEP) dataset, a resource purpose-built to address real-world multi-document summarization (MDS) use cases. Comprising 10,200 distinct event clusters, the dataset includes one human-written reference summary paired with an average of 235 source articles per cluster. The dataset is initially curated from the public Wikipedia Current Events Portal (WCEP)¹. Volunteer WCEP editors produce concise summaries of ongoing and recent news events, alongside a small set of curated links to relevant original source articles. After extracting these ground-truth summaries and their associated limited sets of source articles from the WCEP, we expanded the number of source documents per summary by retrieving semantically and topically matching articles from the Common Crawl News corpus². This curation pipeline yields large clusters of highly redundant news articles that closely resemble the outputs of standard operational news clustering applications. A representative example of an event summary, paired with headlines from both the original WCEP-linked sources and a sample of the additionally retrieved articles, is presented in Table 1. To establish robust baseline performance benchmarks for the dataset, we evaluate a wide range of both unsupervised and supervised MDS methods across our held-out test splits. Our results demonstrate that the expanded set of source articles produces substantially higher upper performance bounds for standard extractive summarization frameworks, and also consistently improves the performance of standard MDS baseline methods.
source_text: In this paper, we present the Wikipedia Current Events Portal (WCEP) dataset, which is designed to address real-world MDS use cases. The dataset consists of 10,200 clusters with one human-written summary and 235 articles per cluster on average. We extract this dataset starting from the Wikipedia Current Events Portal (WCEP) 1 . Editors on WCEP write short summaries about news events and provide a small number of links to relevant source articles. We extract the summaries and source articles from WCEP and increase the number of source articles per summary by searching for similar articles in the Common Crawl News dataset 2 . As a result, we obtain large clusters of highly redundant news articles, resembling the output of news clustering applications. Table 1 shows an example of an event summary, with headlines from both the original article and from a sample of the associated additional sources. In our experiments, we test a range of unsupervised and supervised MDS methods to establish baseline results. We show that the additional articles lead to much higher upper bounds of performance for standard extractive summarization, and help to increase the performance of baseline MDS methods.

text: Given a target bridging anaphor \(a\), the deterministic embedding-based bridging resolution algorithm introduced in Hou (2018a) leverages distributed word representations learned from large text corpora to select the noun phrase (NP) candidate with the strongest semantic relatedness to \(a\) as its antecedent. Predictions produced by this system reflect commonsense world knowledge about paired NP concepts, as the pretrained representations encode distributional patterns tied to real-world conceptual associations. We accordingly use this algorithm to annotate bridging anaphors in the ISNotes corpus: an anaphor is assigned the label *referential bridging/world-knowledge* if it is correctly resolved by the embedding-based bridging system, and the label *referential bridging/context-dependent* otherwise. Table 5 reports a comparison of anaphor resolution accuracy rates across the two bridging categories for two systems: our BARQA model run with gold mention annotations, and the top-performing model from Hou (2018a), denoted MLN II + emb. Note that the MLN II + emb framework incorporates multiple context-level features, such as document span and verb pattern cues. Across all reported results, we observe that our BARQA model achieves stronger performance specifically on the resolution of context-dependent bridging anaphors.
source_text: For a bridging anaphor a, the deterministic algorithm (embeddings bridging) from Hou (2018a) uses a word representation resource learned from a large corpus to predict the most semantically related NP among all NP candidates as the antecedent. The predictions from this system reflect the common-sense world knowledge about the NP pairs. We thus use this algorithm to label bridging anaphors in ISNotes: if a bridging anaphor is correctly resolved by embeddings bridging, we label it as "referential bridging/world-knowledge", otherwise the label is "referential bridging/contextdependent". Table 5 compares the percentage of correctly resolved anaphors between BARQA with gold mentions and the best model from Hou (2018a) (MLN II + emb) on the two bridging categories. Note that MLN II + emb contains several context-level features (e.g., document span, verb pattern). Overall, it seems that our BARQA model is better at resolving context-dependent bridging anaphors.

text: A prominent strand of existing research centers on instance weighting, a technique that assigns domain-specific weights to distinct samples during model training. For example, Wang et al. (2017) integrate both sentence-level weighting and domain-level weighting for NMT tasks. Their sentence-level weight is computed from the bilingual cross-entropy of each sentence pair, calculated against the language model associated with each respective domain. Domain-level weights, in turn, can be adjusted by altering the count of sentences drawn from a given domain within each training mini-batch. Other work proposes a cost weighting method, where the weight assigned to each sentence pair is measured via the output probability of a domain classifier applied to encoder embeddings. Additional studies introduce a dynamic training approach to modulate both sentence selection and weight assignment over the course of training. We note that many of these existing methods are complementary to the model we propose, and can be deployed to improve the training performance of our framework.
source_text: One line of research focuses on instance weighting, which assigns domain related weights to different samples during training. For example, Wang et al. (2017) consider sentence weighting and domain weighting for NMT. The sentence weight is determined by the bilingual cross-entropy of each sentence pair based on the language model of each domain. The domain weight can be modified by changing the number of sentences from that domain in a mini-batch. propose a cost weighting method, where the weight of each pair of sentences is evaluated by the output probability of a domain classifier on the encoder embedding. propose a dynamic training method to adjust the sentence selection and weighting during training. We remark that many of these methods are complementary to our proposed model, and can be applied to improve the training of our model.

text: Incorporating contextualized word representations derived from models such as ELMo and BERT yields substantial performance gains on probing tasks designed to measure capture of named entity and part-of-speech information. This pattern indicates that contextualized word representations encode meaningful syntactic and semantic features relevant to relation extraction (RE), consistent with prior findings including those of Radford et al. (2018), who highlight the effectiveness of linguistic features embedded in contextualized (or deep) language representations for downstream task performance. These observed improvements in capturing core syntactic and semantic properties further correspond to overall gains in RE task performance. Across probing task benchmarks, BERT-based encoders consistently outperform ELMo-based counterparts, delivering more balanced overall performance. This advantage also translates to superior RE accuracy, suggesting that bidirectional language modeling frameworks capture the linguistic properties of input text more effectively. Somewhat counterintuitively, the uncased variant of BERT performs comparably to or better than its cased equivalent on probing tasks targeting entity and part-of-speech information. While this result aligns with expectations for the SemEval dataset, which centers on conceptual semantic relations that do not rely heavily on orthographic cues, the same pattern is unexpected for the TACRED corpus: TACRED explicitly encodes relations between proper entities such as personal names and corporate identifiers, where casing information would intuitively serve as a high-value cue for accurate entity type identification.
source_text: Word Representations Adding contextualized word representations computed by ELMo or BERT greatly increases performance on probing tasks with a focus on named entity and part-of-speech information. This indicates that contextualized word representations encode useful syntactic and semantic features relevant to RE, which is coherent with the findings of and Radford et al. (2018) , who both highlight the effectiveness of linguistic features encoded in contextualized word representations (deep language representations) for downstream tasks. The improved performance on syntactic and semantic abilities is also reflected in an overall improvement in RE task performance. Compared to ELMo, encoders with BERT generally exhibit an overall better and more balanced performance on the probing tasks. This is also reflected in a superior RE performance, suggesting that a bidirectional language model encodes linguistic properties of the input more effectively. Somewhat surprisingly, BERT without casing performs equally or better on the probing tasks focused on entity and part-of-speech information, compared to the cased version. While this intuitively makes sense for SemEval, as the dataset focuses on semantic relations between concepts, it is surprising for TACRED, which contains relations between proper entities, e.g. person and company names, with casing information more important to identify the entity type.

text: A core design property of Universal Dependencies (UD) dependency annotation schemas is the designation of content words as the head units of all dependency relations. Under this framework, function words are uniformly marked as dependents of the content word with which they share the closest syntactic and semantic association. This prioritization of content words surfaces cross-linguistic structural parallels that are far less detectable in alternative annotation schemes, as function word inventories and syntactic behavior exhibit extreme typological variability across languages. Critically, dependency paths connecting pairs of content words almost never include intervening function word nodes as a result of this design choice. Accordingly, cross-linguistic comparison of these content-word dependency paths naturally obscures variation in surface morphosyntactic realization, while highlighting divergent properties of argument structure and inter-constituent linkage across languages.
source_text: An important feature of the dependency analysis in UD is that content words are considered the principal components of dependency relations. Within this framework, function words are generally dependents of the content word they relate to most closely. The primacy of content words brings out cross-linguistic parallels that would be harder to detect with other annotation frameworks since function words are highly variable across languages. Importantly, dependency paths between content words do not generally contain function words. As a result, by comparing paths across languages, differences in the surface realization are often masked, and argument structure and linkage differences emphasized.

text: Several existing directed graph-based chatbot models share core structural parallels with the framework presented in this work, but adopt a drastically distinct approach to the authoring process and are optimized exclusively for chit-chat dialogue desiderata, rather than the general class of interactive fiction (IF). Koller et al. (2018) additionally present a directed graph editing tool for dialogues, with built-in API hooks that enable control of Lego Mindstorms robots. While this use case appears highly dissimilar to our target application at first glance, its underlying data model bears close resemblance to ours. Even the visual similarity of our respective tools alone is sufficient to demonstrate their near-isomorphism: across both systems, the full authoring process is functionally equivalent to defining a valid graph topology and populating pre-defined schemas for each constituent graph component. The core differentiator of our work, by contrast, stems from our incorporation of retrieval-based semantic matching and explicit design focus on general IF use cases.
source_text: directed graph based chatbot model of a similar structure to our own, but approach the authorship process in a drastically different manner and target only the desiderata of chitchat dialog as opposed to the general class of IF. (Koller et al., 2018) also creates a directed graph editing tool for dialogs, with API hooks allowing the control of Lego Mindstorms robots. While this application would at first glance seem quite different, the data model is similar. The visual similarity of our tools alone demonstrates their near isomorphism; in both cases the authorship process is tantamount to the definition of a graph topology and the filling of schema for graph components. The major difference arises instead from our use of retrieval based semantic matching and focus on IF.

text: The complete targeted syntactic evaluation paradigm implemented by SyntaxGym can be fully reproduced outside the platform’s web interface via a dedicated command-line utility, also named syntaxgym, as visualized in Figure 5a. This utility automates end-to-end pipeline operations: it converts standardized test suites into natural language inputs formatted to the specifications of a given target language model, runs inference on the specified model, and maps resulting outputs to a SyntaxGym-compatible structure ready for downstream analysis. The tool natively addresses the substantial cross-model heterogeneity in tokenization strategies and out-of-vocabulary token handling workflows across different language model implementations. Full technical documentation and step-by-step installation instructions are publicly available at syntaxgym.org/cli.
source_text: Users can completely reproduce the targeted syntactic evaluation paradigm of SyntaxGym outside of the website using a second command-line tool, syntaxgym, shown in Figure 5a . This tool does the work of converting test suites into actual natural-language sentences appropriately formatted for a particular language model, executing the model, and mapping the results back to a SyntaxGym-friendly format ready for analysis. It deals with the wide variation in tokenization and out-of-vocabulary token handling across models. Full documentation and installation instructions are available at syntaxgym.org/cli.

text: Lexical divergence is exemplified by the following cross-lingual pair: the English (E) sentence *John broke into the room* maps to the Spanish (S) expression *Juan forzó la entrada al cuarto*, which carries the literal gloss “John forced (the) entry to the room.” Divergences of this type emerge when aligned lexical items exhibit, at most, partially overlapping semantic content, and never appear in isolation, instead always co-occurring with other divergence categories. The data required to ascertain the degree of meaning overlap between paired words is not integrated into Universal Dependencies (UD) or any other cross-lingual annotation scheme; for this reason, we were unable to provide a formal interpretation of this divergence class.
source_text: Lexical divergence: E: John broke into the room ⇔ S: Juan forzó la entrada al cuarto 'John forced (the) entry to the room.' Divergences of this type arise whenever aligned words have at best partially overlapping semantic content and never appear on their own but always with other divergences. The information necessary to ascertain the degree of word-meaning overlap is not embedded into UD or any other cross-lingual annotation scheme; therefore we were unable to provide a formal interpretation of this type of divergence.

text: While self-attention networks (SANs) (Lin et al., 2017) have attained state-of-the-art performance across a range of natural language processing (NLP) tasks (Vaswani et al., 2017; Devlin et al., 2019; Radford et al., 2018), they carry an inherent limitation for sequential modeling stemming from their absence of built-in positional information. To address this gap, absolute position encoding (APE) (Vaswani et al., 2017) and relative position encoding (RPE) (Shaw et al., 2018) have been proposed to more effectively model sequential dependencies. Nevertheless, both absolute and relative position encoding are language-agnostic, with static embeddings that do not adapt across linguistic contexts. This constrains the performance of SANs when modeling multiple languages, which exhibit highly varied word orders and syntactic structures (Gell-Mann and Ruhlen, 2011). Recent studies have demonstrated that explicitly modeling cross-lingual information such as alignment or reordering at either the encoder or attention layer yields improved translation performance across distinct language pairs (Cohn et al., 2016; Du and Way, 2017; Zhao et al., 2018; Kawara et al., 2018). Drawing on these findings, we propose to enhance SANs with cross-lingual representations by encoding reordering indices directly at the embedding layer. As an illustration using the English-to-Chinese translation task, we first rearrange the source English sentence by deriving a latent bracketing transduction grammar (BTG) tree (Wu, 1997), as depicted in Figure 1a. Analogous to absolute position values, this reordering information can be formalized as cross-lingual position, shown in Figure 1b. We further introduce two distinct strategies for integrating cross-lingual position encoding into SAN architectures. We evaluated our approach on three widely cited benchmark datasets for machine translation. Empirical results indicate that leveraging cross-lingual position encoding delivers consistent improvements to translation quality. Additional exploratory analyses show that the proposed method boosts alignment quality (Section 4.3) and performance of the context-free Transformer (Tang et al., 2019) (Section 4.4). Moreover, contrastive evaluation confirms that the observed gains in neural machine translation (NMT) model performance stem from the incorporated cross-lingual information, rather than improved denoising capacity (Section 4.5).
source_text: Although self-attention networks (SANs) (Lin et al., 2017) have achieved the state-of-the-art performance on several natural language processing (NLP) tasks (Vaswani et al., 2017; Devlin et al., 2019; Radford et al., 2018) , they possess the innate disadvantage of sequential modeling due to the lack of positional information. Therefore, absolute position encoding (APE) (Vaswani et al., 2017) and relative position encoding (RPE) (Shaw et al., 2018) were introduced to better capture the sequential dependencies. However, either absolute or relative PE is language-independent and its embedding remains fixed. This inhibits the capacity of SANs when modelling multiple languages, which have diverse word orders and structures (Gell-Mann and Ruhlen, 2011). Recent work have shown that modeling cross-lingual information (e.g., alignment or reordering) at encoder or attention level improves translation performance for different language pairs (Cohn et al., 2016; Du and Way, 2017; Zhao et al., 2018; Kawara et al., 2018) . Inspired by their work, we propose to augment SANs with cross-lingual representations, by encoding reordering indices at embedding level. Taking English⇒Chinese translation task for example, we first reorder the English sentence by deriving a latent bracketing transduction grammar (BTG) tree (Wu, 1997) (Fig. 1a ). Similar to absolute position, the reordering information can be represented as cross-lingual position (Fig. 1b) . In addition, we propose two strategies to incorporate cross-lingual position encoding into SANs. We conducted experiments on three commonlycited datasets of machine translation. Results show that exploiting cross-lingual PE consistently improves translation quality . Further analysis reveals that our method improves the alignment quality ( §Sec. 4.3) and context-free Transformer (Tang et al., 2019) ( §Sec. 4.4) . Furthermore, contrastive evaluation demonstrates that NMT models benefits from the cross-lingual information rather than denoising ability ( §Sec. 4.5).

text: To ensure that observed differences in experimental results are driven solely by variations in training data volume and the exclusive use of representation-level edits rather than structural design choices, we adopt an identical underlying neural architecture to that of our proposed edit model: this backbone consists of a two-layer bidirectional GRU which encodes input sequences of method tokens, coupled with an attention-augmented GRU decoder fitted with a copy mechanism to decode output sequences of comment tokens. We anticipate that the incorporation of more complex architectural designs, including the tree-based encoders proposed in Alon et al. (2019) and graph-based encoders introduced in Fernandes et al. (2019), both of which exploit abstract syntax tree (AST) structure, can be readily applied to both edit and standard generation models, a direction we leave for future investigation.
source_text: We use the same underlying neural architecture as our edit model to make sure that the difference in results comes from the amount of training data and from using edit of representations only: a two-layer, bi-directional GRU that encodes the sequence of tokens in the method, and an attention-based GRU decoder with a copy mechanism that decodes a sequence of comment tokens. We expect the incorporation of more complicated architectures, e.g., tree-based (Alon et al., 2019) and graph-based (Fernandes et al., 2019) encoders which exploit AST structure, can be applied to both an edit model and a generation model, which we leave for future work.

text: This work introduces OpusFilter, a configurable framework for bitext cleaning developed specifically to process datasets retrieved from OPUS (Tiedemann, 2012), the world’s largest public repository of openly available parallel corpora. Unlike preexisting parallel corpus filtering tools such as bicleaner (Sánchez-Cartagena et al., 2018) and Zipporah (Xu and Koehn, 2017), each of which implements a single, fixed filtering approach, OpusFilter is architected as a flexible, modular toolbox that supports both experimentation with and deployment of a diverse range of filtering methodologies. In the sections that follow, we detail the core design of OpusFilter, and demonstrate its practical functionality via a test case involving the filtering of Finnish-English parallel data drawn from the ParaCrawl corpus.
source_text: This paper presents a framework for bitext cleaning, OpusFilter, focusing on processing data collected in OPUS (Tiedemann, 2012) , the world's largest resource of openly available parallel corpora. In contrast to tools such as bicleaner (Sánchez-Cartagena et al., 2018) and Zipporah (Xu and Koehn, 2017) , that implement a single method for parallel corpus filtering, OpusFilter is designed as a toolbox that is useful for testing and using many different approaches. Below we describe the design of OpusFilter and present its application in the test case of filtering Finnish-English parallel data included in ParaCrawl.

text: Broadly speaking, information-seeking applications prioritize the explicit encoding of event information. Existing syntactic representation frameworks typically designate syntactic heads as root predicates, rather than the actual eventive verbs linked to core event semantics. By contrast, the representational approach we propose is centered on the main event predicate of the input sentence, and frames event properties such as aspectuality (exemplified by the sentence *Sam started walking*) and evidentiality (exemplified by *Sam seems to like them*) as modifiers of this core predicate, rather than as syntactic heads themselves. To implement this design in a consistent manner across construction types, we introduce a new node of type STATE for copular sentences, which aligns the event structure of copular constructions with that of clauses containing finite eventive verbs (§4.4).
source_text: In general, information-seeking applications favor making events explicit. Current syntactic representations prefer to assign syntactic heads as root predicates, rather than actual eventive verb. In contrast, we aim to center our representation around the main event predicate in the sentence, while indicating event properties such as aspectuality (Sam started walking) or evidentiality (Sam seems to like them) as modifiers of rather than heads. To do this in a consistent manner, we introduce a new node of type STATE for copular sentences, making their event structure parallel to those containing finite eventive verbs ( §4.4)

text: Among the highest-performing frameworks for the text summarization task is the Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017), which unifies extractive and abstractive modeling approaches using a pointer mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2015) that enables the model to directly copy tokens from the input source text. While copy mechanisms have been broadly adopted across summarization pipelines, reliably ensuring that high-importance tokens from the source are selected for copying remains a persistent open challenge. In our empirical experiments, we observe that transformer-based summarization models equipped with standard copy mechanisms frequently fail to retain certain critical source words. As shown in Table 1, semantically meaningful tokens such as “nominees” and “obama” are explicitly overlooked by the vanilla copy mechanism implementation. To address this gap, we look to the transformer’s self-attention graph to derive informative signals that capture the relative importance of individual source tokens.
source_text: One of the most successful frameworks for the summarization task is Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) that combines extractive and abstractive techniques with a pointer (Vinyals et al., 2015) enabling the model to copy words from the source text directly. Although, copy mechanism has been widely used in summarization task, how to guarantee that important tokens in the source are copied remains a challenge. In our experiments, we find that the transformer-based summarization model with the copy mechanism may miss some important words. As shown in Table 1 , words like "nominees" and "obama" are ignored by the standard copy mechanism. To tackle this problem, we intend to get some clues about the importance of words from the self-attention graph.

text: We first illustrate how meta-linguistic reasoning competencies are deployed to resolve the “Chickasaw puzzle” presented in Table 1. The corresponding translation model is derived via iterative deduction as outlined below: (1) Chickasaw follows a Subject-Object-Verb (SOV) word order, in contrast to the Subject-Verb-Object (SVO) order characteristic of English; (2) nouns carry distinct suffixes depending on whether they occupy a subject or object syntactic position (marked by at and ã, respectively); (3) verbs bear a dedicated suffix to encode 1st person singular pronominal subjects or objects (li and sa, respectively). Critically, it is important to note that the function of the prefix sa, which maps to the English first-person singular object pronoun me, cannot be acquired without first deducing that lhiyohli corresponds to the English verb chases and that third-person agency is not explicitly expressed in Chickasaw. As this example demonstrates, inferring a translation model requires iterative reasoning operating across the levels of words, morphemes, and syntactic abstractions (classes), or, stated alternatively, this process relies on meta-linguistic awareness.
source_text: Let us demonstrate how meta-linguistic reasoning skills are used to solve the "Chickasaw puzzle" given in Table 1 . The translation model is iteratively deduced as follows: (1) the word order in Chickasaw is Subject-Object-Verb (SOV), unlike the English SVO word order; (2) nouns take different suffixes when in a subject or object position (at andã, respectively); (3) verbs take a suffix for 1st person singular pronomial subject or object (li and sa, respectively). Notice that, crucially, it is not possible to learn the function of the prefix sa, which corresponds to me in English, without deducing that lhiyohli corresponds to the verb chases and that third person agency in Chickasaw is not explicitly expressed. As demonstrated, inferring a translation model requires iterative reasoning on the level of words, morphemes and syntactic abstractions (classes), or, to put things differently, it requires meta-linguistic awareness.

text: The performance impacts of distinct DMIN components on the ODIC task are quantified and presented in Table 3, which carries the caption “Ablation study of accuracy (%) on ODIC in a 5-way setup”. Specifically, we perform ablation experiments by removing the DMM and QIM sub-modules, while systematically varying the number of DMR iterations across test conditions. Experimental results indicate that optimal performance is achieved with 3 DMR iterations, and collectively demonstrate the effectiveness of both the dynamic memory module and the query information induction module. For complementary qualitative evaluation, Figure 2 presents the t-distributed stochastic neighbor embedding (t-SNE; Maaten and Hinton, 2008) visualization of support sample vectors before and after DMM processing, generated under a 10-way 5-shot experimental configuration on the ODIC dataset. For this visualization, we randomly select a 50-text support set (10 instances per class) from the held-out ODIC test set, and extract corresponding sample embeddings both pre- and post-DMM processing, denoted as $\{e_{c,s}\}_{c=1,...,5, s=1,...,10}$ and $\{e_{c,s}\}_{c=1,...,5, s=1,...,10}$ respectively. Visual inspection of the t-SNE plots reveals that support vectors output by the DMM exhibit substantially clearer inter-class separation, confirming that the DMM effectively leverages prior supervised learning experience to encode semantic relationships between low-level instance features and high-level class representations for few-shot text classification.
source_text: We analyze the effect of different components of DMIN on ODIC in Table 3 . Specifically, we remove DMM and QIM, and vary the number of DMR iterations. We see that the best performance is achieved with 3 iterations. The results show the effectiveness of both the dynamic memory module and the induction module with query information. Figure 2 is the t-SNE visualization (Maaten and Hinton, 2008) for support sample vectors before Table 3 : Ablation study of accuracy (%) on ODIC in a 5-way setup. and after DMM under a 10-way 5-shot setup on ODIC. We randomly select a support set with 50 texts (10 texts per class) from the ODIC testing set, and obtain the sample vectors before and after DMM, i.e., {e c,s } c=1,...5,s=1...10 and e c,s c=1,...5,s=1...10 . We can see that the support vectors produced by the DMM are better separated, demonstrating the effectiveness of DMM in leveraging the supervised learning experience to encode semantic relationships between lower level instance features and higher level class features for few-shot text classification.

text: The OPUS collection comprises a wide range of distinct corpora, spanning domains from movie subtitles to GNOME interface text. Table 2 reports test BLEU scores for the one-to-many translation task evaluated on the OPUS-100 benchmark, which covers 100 total languages. Abbreviations used in the table are defined as follows: “Bilingual” refers to standard bilingual neural machine translation (NMT) systems; “L” denotes model depth for both the encoder and decoder stacks; “#Param” indicates total model parameter count; “WR” is the percentage win ratio relative to the reference baseline (3); MATT corresponds to the merged attention mechanism introduced in Zhang et al. (2019). LALN and LALT refer to our proposed language-aware layer normalization and language-aware linear transformation modules, respectively. “BLEU94” and “BLEU4” represent the average BLEU score across all 94 translation directions in the test set, and the average BLEU across the En→De, En→Zh, En→Br, and En→Te directions, respectively. Higher values for both BLEU and WR indicate stronger system performance, with top-performing scores for each column highlighted in bold.

text: A number of existing approaches construct date summaries by selecting sentences from pre-ranked candidate lists. This ranking process is grounded in regression models or learning-to-rank frameworks designed to predict ROUGE scores between each individual sentence and a corresponding ground-truth reference summary (Tran et al., 2013a,b). Tran et al. (2015a) observe that end users express a consistent preference for summaries composed of headlines relative to summaries built from sentences pulled directly from source article bodies. Steen and Markert (2019) introduce an abstractive date summarization method underpinned by graph-based sentence merging and compression operations. A separate body of work proposes leveraging supplementary auxiliary data, such as public comments shared on social media platforms (Wang et al., 2015) or visual image content (Wang et al., 2016).
source_text: Several approaches construct date summaries by picking sentences from ranked lists. The ranking is based on regression or learning-to-rank to predict ROUGE scores between the sentence and a ground-truth summary (Tran et al., 2013a,b) . Tran et al. (2015a) observe that users prefer summaries consisting of headlines to summaries consisting of sentences from article bodies. Steen and Markert (2019) propose abstractive date summarization based on graph-based sentence merging and compression. Other works propose the use of additional data, such as comments on social media (Wang et al., 2015 ), or images (Wang et al., 2016 .
